title: Average Order Value
schema: orders(order_id, customer_id, order_total, order_date, status, amount)
solution: SELECT AVG(order_total) AS avg_order_value FROM orders;

title: Find Cheapest Product
schema: products(product_id, name, price, category, sku, manufacturer)
solution: SELECT product_id, name, price FROM products ORDER BY price ASC LIMIT 1;

title: New vs. Returning Customer Report
solution:
SELECT
  CASE WHEN COUNT(*) = 1 THEN 'New' ELSE 'Returning' END AS customer_type,
  COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS customers
FROM (SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id) t
GROUP BY CASE WHEN cnt = 1 THEN 'New' ELSE 'Returning' END;

title: Self-Join for Manager Names
schema: employees(emp_id, name, manager_id, email, hire_date, salary, dept_id, job_title)
solution:
SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager
FROM employees e LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.emp_id;

title: Join Employees and Departments
schema: employees(emp_id, name, dept_id, email, hire_date, salary, manager_id, job_title), departments(dept_id, dept_name, location, manager_id)
solution:
SELECT e.name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;

title: Find Highest Salary
schema: employees(emp_id, name, salary, email, hire_date, dept_id, manager_id, job_title), departments(dept_id, department_name, location, manager_id)
solution: SELECT MAX(salary) AS highest_salary FROM employees;

title: Filter Orders by Date Range
solution: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-02-28';

title: Join Three Tables
schema: orders(customer_id, order_id, order_date, status, amount), customers(customer_id, name, email, signup_date, country, city), products(name, product_id, category, price, sku, manufacturer), order_items(order_id, product_id)
solution:
SELECT o.order_id, c.name AS customer, p.name AS product
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id;

title: Pivot Daily Sales
solution:
SELECT sale_date,
  SUM(CASE WHEN amount > 0 THEN amount END) AS positive_sales,
  COUNT(*) AS txn_count
FROM sales GROUP BY sale_date ORDER BY sale_date;

title: Find Customers Without Orders

title: Use COALESCE for Null Handling
schema: employees(emp_id, name, bonus, email, hire_date, salary, dept_id, manager_id, job_title)
solution: SELECT emp_id, name, COALESCE(bonus, 0) AS bonus FROM employees;

title: Self-Join for Hierarchical Data
schema: employees(emp_id, name, manager_id, hierarchy, level, email, hire_date, salary, dept_id, job_title)
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE hierarchy AS (
  SELECT emp_id, name, manager_id, 1 AS level FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.emp_id, e.name, e.manager_id, h.level+1
  FROM employees e JOIN hierarchy h ON e.manager_id = h.emp_id)
SELECT * FROM hierarchy ORDER BY level, emp_id;

title: Time Difference Between Order Phases

Monthly Hiring Trend by Department
schema: employees(emp_id, dept_id, hire_date, name, email, salary, manager_id, job_title), departments(dept_id, dept_name, location, manager_id)
solution:
SELECT d.dept_name, DATE_TRUNC('month', e.hire_date)::DATE AS month, COUNT(*) AS hires
FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name, DATE_TRUNC('month', e.hire_date)::DATE
ORDER BY month, d.dept_name;

title: Average Order Value by Category with CASE
schema: orders(order_id, category, order_total, customer_id, order_date, status, amount)
solution:
SELECT category,
  AVG(CASE WHEN order_total > 100 THEN order_total END) AS avg_high_value,
  AVG(CASE WHEN order_total <= 100 THEN order_total END) AS avg_low_value

title: Advanced JOIN with Three Tables
schema: customers(customer_id, name, order_id, product_id, qty, email, signup_date, country, city), orders(customer_id, order_id, name, product_id, qty, order_date, status, amount), products(name, product_id, customer_id, order_id, qty, category, price, sku, manufacturer), order_items(order_id, product_id, qty, customer_id, name)
solution:
SELECT c.name, o.order_id, p.name AS product, oi.qty
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id;

title: Overlapping Shift Detector
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS shifts;
CREATE TABLE shifts (
  shift_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  employee_id INT,
  start_time TIMESTAMP,
  end_time TIMESTAMP
);
INSERT INTO shifts VALUES
  (1, 100, '2024-05-01 08:00', '2024-05-01 12:00'),
  (2, 100, '2024-05-01 11:00', '2024-05-01 15:00'),
  (3, 100, '2024-05-01 16:00', '2024-05-01 20:00'),
  (4, 200, '2024-05-02 09:00', '2024-05-02 17:00'),
  (5, 200, '2024-05-02 13:00', '2024-05-02 18:00'),
  (6, 300, '2024-05-03 06:00', '2024-05-03 10:00');

SELECT a.shift_id, b.shift_id
FROM shifts a JOIN shifts b
  ON a.employee_id = b.employee_id AND a.shift_id < b.shift_id
 AND a.start_time < b.end_time AND b.start_time < a.end_time;

title: Self-Join to Identify Missing Supervisors
schema: employees(emp_id, name, supervisor_id, email, hire_date, salary, dept_id, manager_id, job_title)

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS employees;
CREATE TABLE employees (
  emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT,
  supervisor_id INT
);
INSERT INTO employees (emp_id, name, supervisor_id) VALUES
  (1, 'Alice', NULL),
  (2, 'Bob', 1),
  (3, 'Carol', 1),
  (4, 'Dan', 99),
  (5, 'Eve', 2),
  (6, 'Frank', 88),
  (7, 'Grace', 3),
  (8, 'Hank', 77);

SELECT e.emp_id, e.name FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees s ON e.supervisor_id = s.emp_id
WHERE e.supervisor_id IS NOT NULL AND s.emp_id IS NULL;

title: Export SQLite Database Query Results to CSV File
solution:
import sqlite3, csv

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.executescript("""
CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER, name TEXT, amount REAL);
INSERT INTO t VALUES (1,'A',10.5),(2,'B',7.25),(3,'C',99.0);
""")
rows = con.execute('SELECT * FROM t').fetchall()
cols = [d[0] for d in con.execute('SELECT * FROM t').description]

# Write to CSV (in-memory demo).
import io
buf = io.StringIO()
w = csv.writer(buf); w.writerow(cols); w.writerows(rows)
print(buf.getvalue())

title: Find Overlapping Date Ranges
solution:
SELECT a.booking_id, b.booking_id FROM bookings a JOIN bookings b
ON a.room_id = b.room_id AND a.booking_id < b.booking_id
AND a.start_dt ~ '^\d{4}-' AND b.start_dt ~ '^\d{4}-'
AND a.end_dt ~ '^\d{4}-' AND b.end_dt ~ '^\d{4}-'
AND a.start_dt::timestamp < b.end_dt::timestamp AND b.start_dt::timestamp < a.end_dt::timestamp;
_skip_reason: sample-gen crashes on text start_dt/end_dt pair

title: Aggregate Data with CUBE
solution:
SELECT region, product, SUM(amount) AS total FROM sales GROUP BY region, product
UNION ALL
SELECT region, NULL, SUM(amount) FROM sales GROUP BY region
UNION ALL
SELECT NULL, product, SUM(amount) FROM sales GROUP BY product
UNION ALL
SELECT NULL, NULL, SUM(amount) FROM sales
ORDER BY region, product;

title: Set Operation: EXCEPT
Percentile Rank of Sales
solution:

FROM sales;

title: Transaction Anomaly Detector
schema: transactions(txn_id, amount, transaction_id, customer_id, transaction_time, txn_type, status)
solution:
WITH stats AS (
  SELECT AVG(amount) AS mu, AVG(amount*amount) - AVG(amount)*AVG(amount) AS variance
  FROM transactions
)
SELECT t.txn_id, t.amount
FROM transactions t, stats s
WHERE s.variance > 0 AND ABS(t.amount - s.mu) > 3 * SQRT(s.variance);

title: Complex CASE for Bonus Tiers
schema: employees(emp_id, performance, tenure_yrs, salary, name, email, hire_date, dept_id, manager_id, job_title)
solution:
SELECT emp_id,
  CASE WHEN performance='A' AND NULLIF(regexp_replace(tenure_yrs::text, '[^-.0-9]', '', 'g'), '')::numeric >= 5 THEN salary*0.25
       WHEN performance='A' THEN salary*0.15
       WHEN performance='B' AND NULLIF(regexp_replace(tenure_yrs::text, '[^-.0-9]', '', 'g'), '')::numeric >= 5 THEN salary*0.10
       WHEN performance='B' THEN salary*0.05
       ELSE 0 END AS bonus

title: Employee Salary Ranking Analysis
schema: departments(dept_id, department_name, location, manager_id), employees(emp_id, name, email, hire_date, salary, dept_id, manager_id, job_title)
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS employees;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS departments;
CREATE TABLE departments (
    dept_id          INT PRIMARY KEY,
    department_name  TEXT NOT NULL,
    location         TEXT,
    manager_id       INT
);
CREATE TABLE employees (
    emp_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name        TEXT NOT NULL,
    email       TEXT,
    hire_date   DATE,
    salary      NUMERIC NOT NULL,
    dept_id     INT REFERENCES departments,
    manager_id  INT,
    job_title   TEXT
);

-- Departments — manager_id points to the dept head (NULL for empty depts).
-- The dept head is the employee whose own manager_id is NULL in that dept.
INSERT INTO departments (dept_id, department_name, location, manager_id) VALUES
    (1, 'Engineering',     'Mumbai',    1),    -- Alex heads Engineering
    (2, 'Sales',           'New York',  6),    -- Finn heads Sales
    (3, 'Marketing',       'San Jose',  9),    -- Indy heads Marketing (sole employee)
    (4, 'Human Resources', 'Bangalore', NULL), -- empty dept, no manager
    (5, 'Operations',      'Singapore', 10);   -- Jamie heads Operations

-- Employees — manager_id points to the dept head; the dept head themselves has manager_id=NULL.
-- Ties at the top (Alex/Bailey both at $210K) — only Alex is the manager;
-- ties in salary don't imply ties in role.
INSERT INTO employees (emp_id, name, hire_date, salary, dept_id, manager_id, job_title) VALUES
    (1, 'Alex',    '2020-01-10', 210000, 1, NULL, 'Engineering Director'),
    (2, 'Bailey',  '2020-03-15', 210000, 1, 1,    'Principal Engineer'),
    (3, 'Casey',   '2021-06-01', 180000, 1, 1,    'Senior Engineer'),
    (4, 'Drew',    '2021-09-12', 180000, 1, 1,    'Senior Engineer'),
    (5, 'Ellis',   '2022-04-20', 155000, 1, 1,    'Engineer'),
    (6, 'Finn',    '2019-11-05', 240000, 2, NULL, 'Sales Director'),
    (7, 'Gray',    '2020-07-22', 190000, 2, 6,    'Senior AE'),
    (8, 'Harper',  '2022-02-14', 135000, 2, 6,    'SDR'),
    (9, 'Indy',    '2021-12-01', 155000, 3, NULL, 'Head of Marketing'),
    -- HR (dept 4) has no employees — the empty-dept edge case
    (10,'Jamie',   '2019-05-08', 175000, 5, NULL, 'Ops Director'),
    (11,'Kennedy', '2021-01-18', 140000, 5, 10,   'Ops Manager'),
    (12,'Lane',    '2022-08-30', 110000, 5, 10,   'Ops Analyst');

-- The headline query — per-department dense-rank by salary.
-- Edge cases the enrichment walks through:
--   ENG  — ties at top (Alex+Bailey at rk 1, Casey+Drew at rk 2)
--   MKTG — single employee, no "second-highest"
--   HR   — no employees at all
SELECT emp_id, dept_id, salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rk
FROM employees
ORDER BY dept_id, rk, emp_id;
scenario: People-Analytics needs a per-department salary leaderboard for the biannual comp review. The interesting cases to handle: a department where two people are tied at the top (Engineering); a single-person department (Marketing) where 'second-highest' should be NULL not missing; and an empty department (HR) that should still appear in the report. The query needs to surface all three.
question: Given employees(emp_id, dept_id, salary, ...) and departments(dept_id, department_name, ...), rank every employee within their department by salary DESC using DENSE_RANK so ties collapse to one rank. Return emp_id, dept_id, salary, and the dense_rank. The enrichment walks through how to extend this for 'second-highest per department' (Probe 1), 'highest AND second-highest side by side' (Probe 1b), and a rotated pivot with departments as columns (Probe 1c) — including the single-person and empty-department edge cases that motivate the DENSE_RANK + LEFT JOIN pattern.

title: Fetch Paginated API Data and Store in SQLite Database
solution:
import sqlite3

# Pretend each call to fetch_page() hits a paginated API.
# It returns up to `page_size` rows, or [] when exhausted.
_pages = [
    [(1, 'a'), (2, 'b')],
    [(3, 'c')],
    [],  # end-of-stream marker
]
def fetch_page(page):
    return _pages[page] if page < len(_pages) else []

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.execute('CREATE TABLE items (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT)')

page = 0
while True:
    batch = fetch_page(page)
    if not batch: break
    con.executemany('INSERT INTO items VALUES (?, ?)', batch)
    page += 1
con.commit()
print(con.execute('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM items').fetchone()[0], 'rows loaded')

title: SQL JOIN with Pandas Data Processing and CSV Export
solution:
import sqlite3, csv, io

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.executescript("""
CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER, name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE orders    (id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL);
INSERT INTO customers VALUES (1,'Alex'),(2,'Bailey');
INSERT INTO orders    VALUES (10,1,42.0),(11,1,8.0),(12,2,99.5);
""")

q = '''
  SELECT c.id, c.name, SUM(o.amount) AS total
  FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
  GROUP BY c.id, c.name
  ORDER BY total DESC
'''
rows = con.execute(q).fetchall()
cols = [d[0] for d in con.execute(q).description]

buf = io.StringIO()
w = csv.writer(buf); w.writerow(cols); w.writerows(rows)
print(buf.getvalue())

title: Insert New Records into SQLite Database from CSV

import sqlite3, csv, io

CSV_TEXT = 'id,name,price\n1,widget,3.5\n2,gizmo,9.0\n3,gadget,12.75\n'

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.execute('CREATE TABLE products (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, price REAL)')

reader = csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(CSV_TEXT))
con.executemany(
    'INSERT INTO products (id, name, price) VALUES (:id, :name, :price)',
    [{'id': int(r['id']), 'name': r['name'], 'price': float(r['price'])} for r in reader],
)
con.commit()
print(con.execute('SELECT * FROM products').fetchall())

title: Sync Product Catalog from CSV to SQLite Database
solution:
import sqlite3, csv, io

CSV_TEXT = 'product_id,name,price\n1,widget,3.50\n2,gizmo,9.00\n3,gadget,12.75\n'

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.execute('''CREATE TABLE products (
    product_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    price REAL
)''')
con.execute('INSERT INTO products VALUES (1, "old-widget", 1.00)')

for row in csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(CSV_TEXT)):
    con.execute('''
        INSERT INTO products (product_id, name, price)
        VALUES (?, ?, ?)
        ON CONFLICT(product_id) DO UPDATE SET
            name  = excluded.name,
            price = excluded.price
    ''', (int(row['product_id']), row['name'], float(row['price'])))
con.commit()
print(con.execute('SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY product_id').fetchall())

title: Order Time Gap Analysis with LAG
Dynamic SQL Simulation Concept
schema: products(product_id, category, attribute_json, name, price, sku, manufacturer)
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS products;
CREATE TABLE products (
    product_id     INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    category       TEXT NOT NULL,
    attribute_json JSONB NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO products VALUES
  (1, 'phone', '{"screen_size": 6.1, "ram_gb": 8,  "storage_gb": 128}'),
  (2, 'phone', '{"screen_size": 6.7, "ram_gb": 12, "storage_gb": 256}'),
  (3, 'book',  '{"author": "Ursula K. Le Guin", "pages": 254, "isbn": "9780441478125"}'),
  (4, 'book',  '{"author": "N.K. Jemisin",      "pages": 449, "isbn": "9780316229296"}'),
  (5, 'shirt', '{"size": "M", "color": "navy",  "fabric": "cotton"}'),
  (6, 'shirt', '{"size": "L", "color": "olive", "fabric": "cotton-blend"}');

SELECT
    product_id,
    category,
    CASE category
        WHEN 'phone' THEN attribute_json->>'screen_size'
        WHEN 'book'  THEN attribute_json->>'author'
        WHEN 'shirt' THEN attribute_json->>'size'
    END AS primary_attr,
    CASE category
        WHEN 'phone' THEN attribute_json->>'ram_gb'
        WHEN 'book'  THEN attribute_json->>'pages'
        WHEN 'shirt' THEN attribute_json->>'color'
    END AS secondary_attr
FROM products
ORDER BY category, product_id;
question:
A products catalog stores category-specific attributes as a JSON blob in `attribute_json`. Different categories have different keys:

  • phones — screen_size, ram_gb, storage_gb
  • books  — author, pages, isbn
  • shirts — size, color, fabric

Write a SQL query that returns each product with its category-relevant primary and secondary attributes EXTRACTED into named columns. This is the 'dynamic SQL simulation' pattern: in a real app you'd code-generate per-category SELECTs; in pure SQL you simulate the same effect with CASE + json_extract().

Expected output (six rows ordered by category, product_id):

  product_id | category | primary_attr      | secondary_attr
  3          | book     | Ursula K. Le Guin | 254
  4          | book     | N.K. Jemisin      | 449
  1          | phone    | 6.1               | 8
  2          | phone    | 6.7               | 12
  5          | shirt    | M                 | navy
  6          | shirt    | L                 | olive

title: Average Cost per Acquisition
Sum and Average Salaries by Department
schema: employees(emp_id, dept_id, salary, name, email, hire_date, manager_id, job_title)
solution:
WITH d AS (
  SELECT dept_id, AVG(salary) AS mu, AVG(salary*salary) - AVG(salary)*AVG(salary) AS var_pop
  FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id
)
SELECT dept_id, mu AS avg_salary,
  CASE WHEN var_pop > 0 THEN ROUND(mu - SQRT(var_pop), 2) ELSE mu END AS lower_bound,
  CASE WHEN var_pop > 0 THEN ROUND(mu + SQRT(var_pop), 2) ELSE mu END AS upper_bound
FROM d ORDER BY dept_id;

title: Merge Employee and Department Records
schema: employees(emp_id, name, dept_id, dept_name, email, hire_date, salary, manager_id, job_title)
solution:
-- Setup — two source dim tables
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS departments CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE departments (dept_id INT PRIMARY KEY, dept_name TEXT NOT NULL);
INSERT INTO departments VALUES (1,'Engineering'),(2,'Sales'),(3,'Marketing'),(4,'Finance');

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS employees CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE employees (
  emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name   TEXT,
  dept_id INT,
  dept_name TEXT
);
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (101,'Alice',1,NULL),
  (102,'Bob',  2,NULL),
  (103,'Carol',3,'Mktg'),     -- stale dept_name
  (104,'Dave', 9,NULL);       -- dept_id 9 doesn't exist in departments

-- ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Postgres pattern: UPDATE FROM (the MERGE-equivalent)    │
-- │  Sync employees.dept_name from departments.dept_name      │
-- │  whenever the dept_id matches.                            │
-- ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
UPDATE employees e
   SET dept_name = d.dept_name
  FROM departments d
 WHERE e.dept_id = d.dept_id;

-- Show the result
SELECT emp_id, name, dept_id, dept_name FROM employees ORDER BY emp_id;

title: Ranking Sales Representatives Using Dense_Rank
Advanced Hierarchical Data Processing and Recursive Query Optimization for Organizational Structure Analysis
schema: employees(emp_id, name, manager_id, lvl, org, path, text, email, hire_date, salary, dept_id, job_title)
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE org AS (
  SELECT emp_id, name, manager_id, 1 AS lvl, name AS path
  FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.emp_id, e.name, e.manager_id, o.lvl+1, o.path || ' > ' || e.name
  FROM employees e JOIN org o ON e.manager_id = o.emp_id)
SELECT emp_id, name, lvl, path FROM org ORDER BY lvl, name;

title: Prime Batch Warehouse Capacity
Median Search Frequency
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT query, NULLIF(regexp_replace(freq::text, '[^-.0-9]', '', 'g'), '')::numeric AS freq,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY NULLIF(regexp_replace(freq::text, '[^-.0-9]', '', 'g'), '')::numeric) AS rn,
    COUNT(*) OVER () AS n
  FROM searches
)
SELECT AVG(freq) AS median_frequency
FROM ranked
WHERE rn IN (n/2, n/2 + 1, (n+1)/2);

WITH m AS (
  SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', call_at)::DATE AS mo,
    SUM(CASE WHEN duration_sec > 600 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS long_calls
  FROM calls GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', call_at)::DATE)
SELECT mo, long_calls,

FROM m;

title: Reactivated Users Per Month
solution:
WITH gaps AS (
  SELECT user_id, action_date,
    LAG(action_date) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY action_date) AS prev
  FROM (SELECT DISTINCT user_id, action_date FROM activity) d
)
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', action_date)::DATE AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS reactivated
FROM gaps
WHERE (action_date - prev) > 30
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', action_date)::DATE;

title: Senior Manager Direct Reports
schema: employees(emp_id, manager_id, level, name, email, hire_date, salary, dept_id, job_title)
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE chain AS (
  SELECT emp_id, manager_id, 1 AS depth FROM employees WHERE level='Senior Manager'
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.emp_id, e.manager_id, c.depth+1
  FROM employees e JOIN chain c ON e.manager_id = c.emp_id)
SELECT manager_id, COUNT(*) AS reports FROM chain GROUP BY manager_id;

title: Server Fleet Uptime
Combine Data from Multiple Sources into Unified Report
schema: (Python task) merge multiple sources → unified report
solution:
from collections import defaultdict

# Three CSV-style sources to combine
src_a = [{'region': 'NA',   'amount': 100}, {'region': 'NA',   'amount':  50}]
src_b = [{'region': 'EMEA', 'amount':  75}, {'region': 'APAC', 'amount': 125}]
src_c = [{'region': 'APAC', 'amount':  25}, {'region': 'NA',   'amount':  10}]

totals = defaultdict(float)
for row in (*src_a, *src_b, *src_c):
    totals[row['region']] += row['amount']

report = sorted(totals.items(), key=lambda kv: -kv[1])
for region, total in report:
    print(f'{region:6s} {total:8.2f}')

title: Export SQLite Database to Parquet Format with Metadata
solution:
import sqlite3, json, datetime

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.executescript("""
CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER, payload TEXT, created_at TEXT);
INSERT INTO t VALUES (1,'alpha','2025-01-01'), (2,'beta','2025-01-02');
""")

rows = con.execute('SELECT * FROM t').fetchall()
cols = [d[0] for d in con.execute('SELECT * FROM t').description]

# In production: df = pd.read_sql('SELECT * FROM t', con)
#                df.to_parquet('out.parquet', engine='pyarrow', compression='snappy')
# For an in-browser demo we serialize to a metadata-rich JSON envelope.
envelope = {
    'schema': cols,
    'row_count': len(rows),
    'exported_at': datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + 'Z',
    'rows': [dict(zip(cols, r)) for r in rows],
}
print(json.dumps(envelope, indent=2))

title: Identify Overlapping Doctor Appointments
Complex Aggregation and Join: Employee Project Assignments and Salary Calculation
schema: employees(base_salary, emp_id, name, project_id, status, email, hire_date, salary, dept_id, manager_id, job_title), project_assignments(emp_id, project_id, base_salary, name, status), projects(project_id, status, base_salary, emp_id, name, start_date, end_date, manager_id)
solution:
SELECT e.emp_id, e.name,
  COUNT(DISTINCT p.project_id) AS active_projects,
  e.base_salary + 1000 * COUNT(DISTINCT p.project_id) AS total_comp
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN project_assignments pa ON e.emp_id = pa.emp_id
LEFT JOIN projects p ON pa.project_id = p.project_id AND p.status='active'
GROUP BY e.emp_id, e.name, e.base_salary;

title: Complex Data Warehouse Aggregation and Trend Analysis for Multi-Channel Sales Optimization
solution:
WITH monthly AS (
  SELECT channel, DATE_TRUNC('month', sale_date)::DATE AS mo, SUM(amount) AS total
  FROM sales GROUP BY channel, DATE_TRUNC('month', sale_date)::DATE)
SELECT channel, mo, total,
  AVG(total) OVER (PARTITION BY channel ORDER BY mo ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS rolling_3mo,
  100.0*(total-LAG(total) OVER (PARTITION BY channel ORDER BY mo))/NULLIF(LAG(total) OVER (PARTITION BY channel ORDER BY mo),0) AS mom_pct
FROM monthly;

title: Self-Join to Identify Missing Supervisors

solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS employees;
CREATE TABLE employees (emp_id INT, name TEXT, supervisor_id INT);
INSERT INTO employees (emp_id, name, supervisor_id) VALUES
  (1,'Alice',NULL),
  (2,'Bob',1),
  (3,'Carol',1),
  (4,'Dave',2),
  (5,'Eve',99),
  (6,'Frank',88),
  (7,'Grace',2),
  (8,'Hank',77);
SELECT e.emp_id, e.name FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees s ON e.supervisor_id = s.emp_id
WHERE e.supervisor_id IS NOT NULL AND s.emp_id IS NULL;

title: Median Salary by Job Title
schema: employees(job_title, salary, emp_id, name, email, hire_date, dept_id, manager_id)
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT job_title, salary,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY job_title ORDER BY salary) AS rn,
    COUNT(*)    OVER (PARTITION BY job_title)                  AS n
  FROM employees
)
SELECT job_title, AVG(salary) AS median_salary
FROM ranked
WHERE rn IN (n/2, n/2 + 1, (n+1)/2)
GROUP BY job_title
ORDER BY job_title;

title: Merge Employee and Department Records
schema: employees(dept_id, dept_name, departments, emp_id, name, email, hire_date, salary, manager_id, job_title)
solution:
-- Setup — two source dim tables
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS departments CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE departments (dept_id INT PRIMARY KEY, dept_name TEXT NOT NULL);
INSERT INTO departments VALUES (1,'Engineering'),(2,'Sales'),(3,'Marketing'),(4,'Finance');

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS employees CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE employees (
  emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name   TEXT,
  dept_id INT,
  dept_name TEXT
);
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (101,'Alice',1,NULL),
  (102,'Bob',  2,NULL),
  (103,'Carol',3,'Mktg'),     -- stale dept_name
  (104,'Dave', 9,NULL);       -- dept_id 9 doesn't exist in departments

-- ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Postgres pattern: UPDATE FROM (the MERGE-equivalent)    │
-- │  Sync employees.dept_name from departments.dept_name      │
-- │  whenever the dept_id matches.                            │
-- ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
UPDATE employees e
   SET dept_name = d.dept_name
  FROM departments d
 WHERE e.dept_id = d.dept_id;

-- Show the result
SELECT emp_id, name, dept_id, dept_name FROM employees ORDER BY emp_id;

title: CSV and Partitions
schema: orders(order_id BIGINT, region STRING, customer_id BIGINT, amount DECIMAL(12,2), order_ts TIMESTAMP)
question: A 120 GB gzipped CSV of e-commerce orders lands on the lake nightly. Analysts query it 90% of the time with WHERE region IN (...). Convert the CSV to Parquet with a layout that (a) prunes by region at read time, and (b) produces a sensible number of files per region (not 1 per task, not 1 monster file). The column `region` has 8 distinct values (US-E, US-W, EU, UK, IN, APAC, LATAM, MEA) and is skewed: US-E ~38%, US-W ~22%. Use the right combination of repartition() and partitionBy() and name the trade-off each makes.
solution:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession, functions as F
from pyspark.sql import Row

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("csv-and-partitions").getOrCreate()

# Seeded sample (in production: spark.read.option('header',True).schema(...).csv('/data/in.csv'))
orders = spark.createDataFrame([
    Row(order_id=1, region="US-E",  customer_id=10, amount=49.99),
    Row(order_id=2, region="US-E",  customer_id=11, amount=12.50),
    Row(order_id=3, region="EU",    customer_id=12, amount=88.00),
    Row(order_id=4, region="APAC",  customer_id=13, amount= 7.20),
    Row(order_id=5, region="LATAM", customer_id=14, amount=21.10),
    Row(order_id=6, region="US-W",  customer_id=15, amount=33.00),
])

# repartition(N, col) sets in-memory shuffle layout: each task holds one region's rows.
# partitionBy(col) sets on-disk directory layout for partition pruning at read time.
# Together: one file per region directory, downstream WHERE region='EU' reads ONE folder.
(orders.repartition(8, F.col("region"))
       .write.mode("overwrite")
       .partitionBy("region")
       .parquet("/tmp/orders_out"))

# Guard against silently wiping unrelated partitions on next run:
# spark.conf.set('spark.sql.sources.partitionOverwriteMode', 'dynamic')

title: Repartition
schema: events(user_id BIGINT, action STRING, amount DOUBLE)
question: Given an `events` DataFrame, show the three canonical uses of repartition/coalesce: (A) increase parallelism before a heavy groupBy on user_id, (B) collapse to a small number of output files before a downstream BI tool reads them, (C) pre-co-partition two DataFrames so a join avoids a shuffle. For each, name WHY you chose repartition vs coalesce, and why N=8 (the cluster size in this example).
solution:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession, functions as F, Row

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("repartition-vs-coalesce").getOrCreate()

events = spark.createDataFrame([
    Row(user_id=1, action="login", amount=10.0),
    Row(user_id=1, action="buy",   amount=42.0),
    Row(user_id=2, action="login", amount=10.0),
    Row(user_id=3, action="buy",   amount=99.0),
    Row(user_id=4, action="buy",   amount=18.0),
    Row(user_id=5, action="buy",   amount=77.0),
])

# A) repartition(N, col) = FULL SHUFFLE, hash-partitioned by col.
#    Use BEFORE a heavy groupBy(col): Catalyst sees co-located data and skips its own shuffle.
for_agg = events.repartition(8, F.col("user_id"))
totals = for_agg.groupBy("user_id").agg(F.sum("amount").alias("total"))

# B) coalesce(N) = NO SHUFFLE. Merges adjacent partitions. Shrink-only.
#    Use to collapse output file count. Back-propagates: upstream stage runs on N tasks too.
totals.coalesce(1).write.mode("overwrite").parquet("/tmp/user_totals")

# C) Pre-join co-partitioning: repartition BOTH sides on join key with SAME N.
N = 8
orders    = spark.createDataFrame([Row(order_id=i, amount=i*10.0) for i in range(20)])
shipments = spark.createDataFrame([Row(order_id=i, carrier="UPS")  for i in range(20)])
joined = (orders.repartition(N, F.col("order_id"))
          .join(shipments.repartition(N, F.col("order_id")), "order_id"))
joined.explain()   # Look for no Exchange on join's children = shuffle-free join.

title: Broadcast Join
schema: orders(order_id BIGINT, country_code STRING, amount DECIMAL(12,2)); dim_country(country_code STRING, country_name STRING, region STRING, currency STRING)
question: Enrich a 1-billion-row `orders` fact (~400 GB) with a 249-row `dim_country` lookup. Default SortMergeJoin shuffles 400 GB across the network — a 4-hour job. Use broadcast hash join to replace the shuffle with an in-executor hash lookup, naming the broadcast threshold, AQE behaviour, and the join type → broadcastable side rules.
solution:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession, functions as F, Row
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

spark = (SparkSession.builder.appName("broadcast-join")
         .config("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold", "10485760")   # 10 MB default
         .config("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
         .getOrCreate())

orders = spark.createDataFrame([
    Row(order_id=1, country_code="US", amount=49.99),
    Row(order_id=2, country_code="IN", amount= 7.20),
    Row(order_id=3, country_code="DE", amount=88.00),
    Row(order_id=4, country_code="XX", amount= 5.00),   # unmatched → tests LEFT semantics
])
dim_country = spark.createDataFrame([
    Row(country_code="US", country_name="United States", region="NA",   currency="USD"),
    Row(country_code="IN", country_name="India",         region="APAC", currency="INR"),
    Row(country_code="DE", country_name="Germany",       region="EU",   currency="EUR"),
])

# broadcast() hints the planner: ship the right side to every executor as a hash map.
# Converts SortMergeJoin -> BroadcastHashJoin. No shuffle on the LEFT side.
# Practical limit ~200 MB; driver heap must hold the build side during collect.
joined = orders.join(broadcast(dim_country), "country_code", "left")

joined.explain()    # Verify physical plan shows BroadcastHashJoin, not SortMergeJoin.
joined.show(truncate=False)

title: Cache and Performance
schema: raw_events(event_id BIGINT, user_id BIGINT, country STRING, device STRING, ts TIMESTAMP, amount DOUBLE)
question: A 22-minute lineage produces an `enriched_events` DataFrame consumed by 4 downstream writes in the same job. Without caching, Spark re-executes the lineage 4 times = 88 minutes. Cache it correctly: pick the right storage level for PySpark, materialise before the consumers branch out, and unpersist when done. Name what `cache()` does vs `persist()` vs `checkpoint()` and when each one wins.
solution:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession, functions as F, Row
from pyspark import StorageLevel

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("cache-and-performance").getOrCreate()

raw_events = spark.createDataFrame([
    Row(event_id=1, user_id=10, country="US", device="ios",     ts="2026-01-01 09:01", amount= 12.0),
    Row(event_id=2, user_id=11, country="US", device="android", ts="2026-01-01 09:02", amount= 88.0),
    Row(event_id=3, user_id=12, country="DE", device="ios",     ts="2026-01-01 09:05", amount=  5.0),
    Row(event_id=4, user_id=10, country="US", device="ios",     ts="2026-01-01 10:01", amount=110.0),
    Row(event_id=5, user_id=13, country="IN", device="android", ts="2026-01-01 10:15", amount=  3.5),
])

# Stand-in for an expensive 18-stage lineage.
enriched = (raw_events.withColumn("ts", F.to_timestamp("ts"))
                      .withColumn("hour", F.hour("ts"))
                      .withColumn("amount_usd", F.col("amount") * 1.0))

# MEMORY_AND_DISK = safe PySpark default. Spills to disk under RAM pressure instead
# of silently re-computing partitions (which is what MEMORY_ONLY does on eviction).
enriched.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)

# Materialise eagerly. cache() is lazy; without an action, the first consumer races the fill.
enriched.count()

# Fan out — each write now reads from the warm cache, not the 22-min lineage.
enriched.groupBy("country").agg(F.sum("amount_usd").alias("total")).write.mode("overwrite").parquet("/tmp/by_country")
enriched.groupBy("device").agg(F.count("*").alias("events")).write.mode("overwrite").parquet("/tmp/by_device")
enriched.groupBy("hour").agg(F.avg("amount_usd").alias("avg")).write.mode("overwrite").parquet("/tmp/by_hour")
enriched.filter(F.col("amount_usd") > 100).write.mode("overwrite").parquet("/tmp/fraud_signals")

# Pair persist/unpersist like malloc/free. Long-lived sessions OOM without this.
enriched.unpersist(blocking=True)

title: Window Functions without Partitions
schema: transactions(txn_id BIGINT, account_id STRING, ts TIMESTAMP, amount DOUBLE)
question: A `transactions` DataFrame has 1.2B rows (~180 GB). The naive prompt asks for a global running total via `Window.orderBy('ts')` with no partitionBy — which collapses all data to a single executor, triggers a Spark warning, and OOMs at production scale. Show (A) the safe per-account version (parallel by design), and (B) the two-pass associative pattern (repartitionByRange + local cumsum + offset join) for when a truly global running total is required.
solution:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession, Window, functions as F, Row

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("window-without-partition").getOrCreate()

txns = spark.createDataFrame([
    Row(txn_id=1, account_id="A", ts="2026-01-01 09:00", amount=100.0),
    Row(txn_id=2, account_id="A", ts="2026-01-01 09:05", amount= 50.0),
    Row(txn_id=3, account_id="B", ts="2026-01-01 09:10", amount=200.0),
    Row(txn_id=4, account_id="A", ts="2026-01-01 09:15", amount= 25.0),
    Row(txn_id=5, account_id="B", ts="2026-01-01 09:20", amount= 75.0),
]).withColumn("ts", F.to_timestamp("ts"))

# A) SAFE: per-account running total. Parallel by design — each account is one task,
#    accounts spread across the cluster. This is what the analytical question almost always is.
w = Window.partitionBy("account_id").orderBy("ts") \
          .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)
per_account = (txns.withColumn("rn",            F.row_number().over(w))
                   .withColumn("running_total", F.sum("amount").over(w)))
per_account.show()

# B) Two-pass GLOBAL running total (only when truly needed). sum() is associative.
#    Step 1: range-partition so each partition holds a contiguous time slice.
K = 4
ranged = txns.repartitionByRange(K, "ts").withColumn("pid", F.spark_partition_id())

#    Step 2: local cumsum inside each partition (parallel, no shuffle).
w_local = Window.partitionBy("pid").orderBy("ts").rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)
locally = ranged.withColumn("local_running", F.sum("amount").over(w_local))

#    Step 3: cumulative offset of all earlier partitions (K rows, runs on 1 task safely).
w_off = Window.orderBy("pid").rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, -1)
offsets = (locally.groupBy("pid").agg(F.sum("amount").alias("part_sum"))
                  .withColumn("offset", F.coalesce(F.sum("part_sum").over(w_off), F.lit(0.0)))
                  .select("pid", "offset"))

#    Step 4: global = local + offset.
global_running = (locally.join(offsets, "pid")
                  .withColumn("running_total", F.col("local_running") + F.col("offset"))
                  .drop("pid", "local_running"))
global_running.orderBy("ts").show()

title: Two Sum
Valid Palindrome
Valid Parentheses
Binary Search
solution:
def search(nums, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(nums)-1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo+hi)//2
        if nums[mid] == target: return mid
        if nums[mid] < target: lo = mid+1
        else: hi = mid-1
    return -1

title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
solution:
def mergeTwoLists(a, b):
    dummy = tail = ListNode()
    while a and b:
        if a.val <= b.val: tail.next, a = a, a.next
        else:              tail.next, b = b, b.next
        tail = tail.next
    tail.next = a or b
    return dummy.next

title: Invert Binary Tree

def invertTree(root):
    if not root: return None
    root.left, root.right = invertTree(root.right), invertTree(root.left)
    return root

    l, r = 0, len(numbers)-1
    while l < r:

        if s < target:  l += 1
        else:           r -= 1

title: Three Sum
solution:
def threeSum(nums):
    nums.sort(); res = []
    for i, a in enumerate(nums):
        if i and nums[i] == nums[i-1]: continue
        l, r = i+1, len(nums)-1
        while l < r:
            s = a + nums[l] + nums[r]
            if s == 0:
                res.append([a, nums[l], nums[r]])
                l += 1
                while l < r and nums[l] == nums[l-1]: l += 1
            elif s < 0: l += 1
            else:       r -= 1
    return res

title: Container With Most Water
solution:
def maxArea(h):
    l, r, best = 0, len(h)-1, 0
    while l < r:
        best = max(best, (r-l) * min(h[l], h[r]))
        if h[l] < h[r]: l += 1
        else:           r -= 1
    return best

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
solution:
def lengthOfLongestSubstring(s):
    last, l, best = {}, 0, 0
    for r, c in enumerate(s):
        if c in last and last[c] >= l: l = last[c] + 1
        last[c] = r
        best = max(best, r-l+1)
    return best

from collections import Counter
def checkInclusion(s1, s2):
    need, win = Counter(s1), Counter()
    k = len(s1)
    for i, c in enumerate(s2):
        win[c] += 1
        if i >= k: 
            win[s2[i-k]] -= 1
            if win[s2[i-k]] == 0: del win[s2[i-k]]
        if win == need: return True
    return False

def searchMatrix(M, target):
    if not M: return False
    m, n = len(M), len(M[0])
    lo, hi = 0, m*n-1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo+hi)//2
        v = M[mid//n][mid%n]
        if v == target: return True
        if v < target: lo = mid+1
        else: hi = mid-1
    return False

def minEatingSpeed(piles, h):
    lo, hi = 1, max(piles)
    while lo < hi:
        m = (lo+hi)//2
        if sum((p+m-1)//m for p in piles) <= h: hi = m
        else: lo = m+1
    return lo

    lo, hi = 0, len(nums)-1
    while lo < hi:
        m = (lo+hi)//2
        if nums[m] > nums[hi]: lo = m+1
        else: hi = m
    return nums[lo]

def removeNthFromEnd(head, n):
    dummy = ListNode(0, head); fast = slow = dummy
    for _ in range(n): fast = fast.next
    while fast.next: fast, slow = fast.next, slow.next
    slow.next = slow.next.next
    return dummy.next

def addTwoNumbers(l1, l2):
    dummy = cur = ListNode(); carry = 0
    while l1 or l2 or carry:
        s = (l1.val if l1 else 0) + (l2.val if l2 else 0) + carry
        carry, v = divmod(s, 10)
        cur.next = ListNode(v); cur = cur.next
        l1 = l1.next if l1 else None
        l2 = l2.next if l2 else None
    return dummy.next

solution:
def combinationSum(candidates, target):
    res = []
    def back(i, path, total):
        if total == target: res.append(path[:]); return
        if total > target or i == len(candidates): return
        path.append(candidates[i]); back(i, path, total+candidates[i]); path.pop()
        back(i+1, path, total)
    back(0, [], 0); return res

Graph Valid Tree
solution:
def validTree(n, edges):
    if len(edges) != n-1: return False
    parent = list(range(n))
    def find(x):
        while parent[x] != x: parent[x] = parent[parent[x]]; x = parent[x]
        return x
    for a, b in edges:
        ra, rb = find(a), find(b)
        if ra == rb: return False
        parent[ra] = rb
    return True

title: Network Delay Time

Gas Station
solution:
def canCompleteCircuit(gas, cost):
    if sum(gas) < sum(cost): return -1
    tank = start = 0
    for i in range(len(gas)):
        tank += gas[i] - cost[i]
        if tank < 0: start = i+1; tank = 0
    return start

title: Merge Customer Records from Two Sources
schema: customers(customer_id, email, name, updated_at, staging_customers, signup_date, country, city)
solution:
-- Setup — target customers + staging_customers (incoming changes)
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS customers CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE customers (
  customer_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT, email TEXT, updated_at TIMESTAMP
);
INSERT INTO customers VALUES
  (1,'Alice','alice@old.com','2025-01-01 09:00'),
  (2,'Bob',  'bob@old.com',  '2025-01-15 09:00'),
  (3,'Carol','carol@old.com','2025-02-01 09:00');

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS staging_customers CASCADE;
CREATE TABLE staging_customers (
  customer_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT, email TEXT, updated_at TIMESTAMP
);
INSERT INTO staging_customers VALUES
  (2,'Bobby',     'bob@new.com',     '2025-04-01 12:00'),  -- update — newer
  (3,'Carol',     'carol@old.com',   '2024-12-01 09:00'),  -- update — older (skip)
  (4,'Dave',      'dave@new.com',    '2025-04-01 12:00');  -- insert

-- ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Postgres MERGE-equivalent: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT        │
-- │  customer_id is the conflict key; updated_at gates the    │
-- │  UPDATE so older staging rows don't overwrite.            │
-- ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
INSERT INTO customers (customer_id, name, email, updated_at)
SELECT customer_id, name, email, updated_at FROM staging_customers
ON CONFLICT (customer_id) DO UPDATE
   SET name       = EXCLUDED.name,
       email      = EXCLUDED.email,
       updated_at = EXCLUDED.updated_at
 WHERE EXCLUDED.updated_at > customers.updated_at;

SELECT customer_id, name, email, updated_at FROM customers ORDER BY customer_id;

def copyRandomList(head):
    if not head: return None
    m = {}
    cur = head
    while cur: m[cur] = Node(cur.val); cur = cur.next
    cur = head
    while cur:
        m[cur].next   = m.get(cur.next)
        m[cur].random = m.get(cur.random)
        cur = cur.next
    return m[head]

title: Reorder List
solution:
def reorderList(head):
    # find middle
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next: slow, fast = slow.next, fast.next.next
    # reverse second half
    prev, cur = None, slow.next; slow.next = None
    while cur: cur.next, prev, cur = prev, cur, cur.next
    # merge
    a, b = head, prev
    while b:
        a.next, b.next, a, b = b, a.next, a.next, b.next

Sliding Window Median
solution:
from sortedcontainers import SortedList
def medianSlidingWindow(nums, k):
    sl = SortedList(nums[:k]); res = []
    def med(): return (sl[k//2] + sl[(k-1)//2]) / 2
    res.append(med())
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        sl.add(nums[i]); sl.remove(nums[i-k])
        res.append(med())
    return res

title: Subarray Sum Equals K

from collections import defaultdict
def subarraySum(nums, k):
    cnt = defaultdict(int); cnt[0] = 1
    s = res = 0
    for n in nums:
        s += n
        res += cnt[s - k]
        cnt[s] += 1
    return res

Log Aggregator
solution:
from collections import defaultdict
import random

class LogAggregator:
    """Folds a firehose of (ts, key, value) events into fixed-width time
    buckets. add / query / top are the primitives that every metrics pipeline
    exposes -- Datadog, Prometheus, CloudWatch, Kafka Streams windowed counts."""
    def __init__(self, bucket_seconds=60):
        self.buckets = defaultdict(int)
        self.bs = bucket_seconds

    def add(self, ts, key, val=1):
        self.buckets[(ts // self.bs, key)] += val

    def query(self, bucket_id, key):
        return self.buckets.get((bucket_id, key), 0)

    def top(self, bucket_id, k=10):
        items = [(key, v) for (b, key), v in self.buckets.items() if b == bucket_id]
        return sorted(items, key=lambda x: -x[1])[:k]


if __name__ == "__main__":
    random.seed(11)
    start_ts, BUCKET = 1_700_000_000, 60
    agg = LogAggregator(bucket_seconds=BUCKET)
    hosts = [f"web-{i:02d}" for i in range(10)]
    sick = "web-07"  # degraded disk: source of 95% of ERRORs

    # 10 minutes, 10k log lines / minute cluster-wide
    for minute in range(10):
        for _ in range(10_000):
            ts = start_ts + minute * 60 + random.randint(0, 59)
            if random.random() < 0.95:
                host = sick if random.random() < 0.95 else random.choice(hosts)
                level = "ERROR" if random.random() < 0.20 else "INFO"
            else:
                host = random.choice(hosts)
                level = "INFO"
            agg.add(ts, key=(level, host))

    m5 = (start_ts + 5 * 60) // BUCKET
    print("ERROR web-07 in minute 5:", agg.query(m5, ("ERROR", "web-07")))
    print("ERROR web-02 in minute 5:", agg.query(m5, ("ERROR", "web-02")))
    print("Top 5 in minute 5:")
    for k, v in agg.top(m5, k=5):
        print(" ", k, "->", v)

    # Cluster-level: 95%+ of ERRORs come from one host -- the bad disk.
    total = sum(v for (b, (lvl, h)), v in agg.buckets.items() if lvl == "ERROR")
    sick_n = sum(v for (b, (lvl, h)), v in agg.buckets.items()
                 if lvl == "ERROR" and h == sick)
    print(f"Total ERRORs (10 min): {total}; on {sick}: {sick_n} "
          f"({100 * sick_n / total:.1f}%)")
question: Design an in-memory log aggregator that ingests a firehose of (timestamp, key, value) tuples and answers three primitive queries used by every observability dashboard: add(ts, key, val) -- record an event; query(bucket_id, key) -- get the rolled-up count for one (time-bucket, key) pair; top(bucket_id, k) -- return the k busiest keys in a given bucket. Time buckets are fixed-width (e.g., 60-second). Demonstrate with a seeded fleet of 10 web servers emitting 10,000 log lines/min, where one host is the source of 95% of ERROR-level lines, that the aggregator turns a 100k-line firehose into ~30 hash entries per minute and that top() immediately surfaces the offending host.

title: Event Stream Deduplicator
Skew-Aware Key Partitioner
solution:
import hashlib
import random
from collections import Counter

class SkewAwarePartitioner:
    """Hash-partitions events across N partitions, with optional salting for
    known hot keys to prevent any single partition from being overloaded.

    Use case: Kafka topic partitioning, DynamoDB partition key generation,
    Spark / Flink keyed-state distribution, Cassandra composite-partition-key
    bucketing. Same pattern, different substrate.
    """
    def __init__(self, n_partitions, hot_keys, salt_factor=8):
        self.n = n_partitions
        self.hot = set(hot_keys)
        self.salt = salt_factor

    def partition(self, key, row_index=0):
        # Hot keys get a salt suffix; cold keys hash unchanged.
        k = f"{key}#{row_index % self.salt}" if key in self.hot else key
        return int(hashlib.md5(k.encode()).hexdigest(), 16) % self.n


if __name__ == "__main__":
    random.seed(7)
    # One whale (1000 events), 100 dolphins (10 each), 1000 minnows (1 each).
    stream = (
        [("user_42", i) for i in range(1000)] +
        [(f"u{j:03d}", i) for j in range(100) for i in range(10)] +
        [(f"m{k:04d}", 0) for k in range(1000)]
    )
    random.shuffle(stream)

    # BEFORE: naive partitioner, no hot-key awareness.
    naive = SkewAwarePartitioner(n_partitions=16, hot_keys=set())
    nl = Counter(naive.partition(k, i) for k, i in stream)
    print("Naive  -> max:", max(nl.values()), " min:", min(nl.values()),
          " skew:", round(max(nl.values())/min(nl.values()), 2), "x")
    # Naive  -> max: 1107  min: 81  skew: 13.67x

    # AFTER: salt the known whale across 8 buckets.
    aware = SkewAwarePartitioner(n_partitions=16, hot_keys={"user_42"}, salt_factor=8)
    al = Counter(aware.partition(k, i) for k, i in stream)
    print("Aware  -> max:", max(al.values()), " min:", min(al.values()),
          " skew:", round(max(al.values())/min(al.values()), 2), "x")
    # Aware  -> max: 390   min: 81  skew:  4.81x
question: Design a partitioner for a Kafka-like topic with N partitions where you know a small set of "hot" keys produce disproportionately more events than the rest. Naive hash partitioning pins each hot key to one partition, creating a hot shard that consumers can't keep up with. Implement SkewAwarePartitioner(n_partitions, hot_keys, salt_factor) with a partition(key, row_index) method that returns a partition id in [0, n_partitions). Hot keys should fan out across `salt_factor` partitions by appending a salt suffix to the key before hashing; cold keys should hash unchanged. Then demonstrate with a seeded stream that the max-to-min partition-load ratio drops materially when the salt is applied.
