    FROM islands GROUP BY customer_id
),
idle_gaps AS (
    SELECT customer_id,
           m_idx - LAG(m_idx) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY m_idx) - 1 AS idle_months
    FROM indexed
),
idle_per_cust AS (
    SELECT customer_id, COALESCE(MAX(idle_months), 0) AS max_idle_months
    FROM idle_gaps GROUP BY customer_id
),
agg AS (
    SELECT e.customer_id,
           MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date,
           MAX(order_date) AS last_order_date,
           COUNT(DISTINCT ym) AS months_active,
           COUNT(*) AS lifetime_orders,
           ROUND(SUM(amount), 2) AS lifetime_spend,
           (MAX(order_date) - MIN(order_date)) AS tenure_days,
           ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 365 THEN amount ELSE 0 END), 2) AS y1_spend,
           ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN days_back >= 365 AND days_back < 730 THEN amount ELSE 0 END), 2) AS y2_spend,
           ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 90 THEN amount ELSE 0 END), 2) AS q1_spend,
           ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN days_back >= 90 AND days_back < 180 THEN amount ELSE 0 END), 2) AS q2_spend,
           ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN days_back >= 180 AND days_back < 270 THEN amount ELSE 0 END), 2) AS q3_spend,
           ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN days_back >= 270 AND days_back < 365 THEN amount ELSE 0 END), 2) AS q4_spend,
           ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN amount ELSE 0 END), 2) AS l28_spend,
           ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN days_back >= 28 AND days_back < 56 THEN amount ELSE 0 END), 2) AS prior_l28_spend
    FROM e GROUP BY e.customer_id
),
joined AS (
    SELECT a.*, sp.longest_streak, ip.max_idle_months
    FROM agg a
    JOIN streak_per_cust sp ON sp.customer_id = a.customer_id
    JOIN idle_per_cust   ip ON ip.customer_id = a.customer_id
)
SELECT customer_id, first_order_date, last_order_date,
       months_active, longest_streak, max_idle_months,
       lifetime_orders, lifetime_spend, y1_spend, y2_spend,
       CASE WHEN y2_spend > 0 THEN ROUND(100.0*(y1_spend - y2_spend)/y2_spend, 1) END AS yoy_growth_pct,
       q1_spend, q2_spend, q3_spend, q4_spend,
       l28_spend, prior_l28_spend,
       CASE WHEN prior_l28_spend > 0 THEN ROUND(l28_spend/prior_l28_spend, 2) END AS l28_velocity,
       DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY y1_spend DESC) AS loyalty_rank
FROM joined
WHERE tenure_days >= 730 AND months_active >= 12 AND longest_streak >= 6
ORDER BY y1_spend DESC, yoy_growth_pct DESC;

title: Customer Growth Accounting — New, Resurrected, Existing, Churned
scenario:
Customer growth accounting (Stripe / Jeff Sauro framework) decomposes every active customer in a given month into one of four buckets so the team can read the health of the business from a single table:
  • New        — first ever order this month
  • Resurrected — active this month, active in some prior month, but NOT last month
  • Existing   — active this month AND active last month
  • Churned    — NOT active this month, but active last month (counted negatively)

The Quick Ratio = (New + Resurrected) / Churned. > 1 means the store is growing.
question:
Produce one row per (year_month, bucket) with:
  year_month  | bucket       | customers
where bucket ∈ ('new', 'resurrected', 'existing', 'churned'). Customers count is positive for new/resurrected/existing, and we report churned as a separate positive number (the consumer can negate it).
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id    INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    order_date  TEXT    NOT NULL,
    amount      REAL    NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (1, 1, '2025-01-05', 100), (2, 1, '2025-02-08', 100),
  (3, 1, '2025-03-12', 100),
  (4, 2, '2025-01-20',  80), (5, 2, '2025-03-15',  80),
  (6, 3, '2025-02-18',  50),
  (7, 4, '2025-03-22', 200);

-- ── PERF rewrite ─────────────────────────────────────────
--   1. GROUP BY user_id, ym instead of SELECT DISTINCT — early-
--      aggregation pushdown in MPP optimisers. Critical when orders
--      has billions of rows and the customer × ym grid is small.
--   2. Date-range WHERE for partition pruning. The classifier needs
--      one extra month before the report window so prev_m / next_m
--      LAG/LEAD aren't NULL inside the report range.
-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WITH user_activity AS (
    SELECT customer_id, strftime('%Y-%m', order_date) AS ym
    FROM orders
    WHERE order_date >= '2024-12-01'  -- one month buffer for LAG/LEAD
      AND order_date <  '2025-05-01'
    GROUP BY customer_id, strftime('%Y-%m', order_date)
),
edges AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        ym,
        date(ym || '-01') AS current_m,
        LAG (date(ym || '-01')) OVER w AS prev_m,
        LEAD(date(ym || '-01')) OVER w AS next_m
    FROM user_activity
    WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY ym)
),
classifications AS (
    SELECT
        ym, customer_id,
        CASE
            WHEN prev_m IS NULL                       THEN 'new'
            WHEN prev_m = date(current_m, '-1 month') THEN 'existing'
            ELSE                                            'resurrected'
        END AS bucket,
        CASE
            WHEN next_m IS NULL OR next_m > date(current_m, '+1 month')
            THEN strftime('%Y-%m', date(current_m, '+1 month'))
        END AS churn_ym
    FROM edges
)
SELECT ym AS year_month, bucket, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS customers
FROM (
    SELECT ym, customer_id, bucket FROM classifications
    UNION ALL
    SELECT churn_ym, customer_id, 'churned'
    FROM classifications WHERE churn_ym IS NOT NULL
)
GROUP BY ym, bucket
ORDER BY ym,
    CASE bucket WHEN 'new' THEN 1 WHEN 'resurrected' THEN 2
                WHEN 'existing' THEN 3 ELSE 4 END;

title: Resurrected Customers — Cycle Count, AOV Uplift, Y1 vs Y2 Resurrection Rate
subtopic: LAG · multi-cycle resurrection · AOV uplift · Y1/Y2
schema: orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status), params(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount)
scenario: Real customers don't churn-and-resurrect once — they yo-yo. We model each customer's order stream as a sequence of 'active phases' (islands) separated by churn gaps (> 90 days). The right marketing call depends on more than 'did they resurrect': a customer on their fifth phase with shrinking phase-AOV is a different bet than a Tier 1 customer on their first 90-day wobble. The report should also show how Y1 (rolling 12 months) compares to Y2 (the 12 months before that) — a rising resurrection rate is a leading indicator that the product is losing stickiness.
question:
Snapshot 2025-04-01. Use the gaps-and-islands pattern: assign each order an island_id that bumps every time the previous-order gap exceeds 90 days. For each customer with at least 2 islands (i.e. at least 1 resurrection cycle), return:
  customer_id
  total_islands            — number of distinct active phases
  resurrection_cycles      — total_islands - 1
  avg_gap_days             — average idle gap between islands
  max_gap_days             — longest idle gap
  avg_pre_churn_aov        — AOV of last order in each closing island
  avg_comeback_aov         — AOV of first order in each new island
  aov_uplift               — comeback - pre_churn (positive = healthy)
  y1_resurrections         — comeback events in the rolling-12 window
  y2_resurrections         — comeback events in the prior-12 window
  resurrection_trend       — 'rising','falling','stable','y1_only'
  most_recent_comeback     — date

Sort by resurrection_cycles DESC, aov_uplift DESC.
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    order_date DATE NOT NULL,
    amount NUMERIC NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (1,1,'2023-04-10',100),(2,1,'2023-04-25',120),
  (3,1,'2023-08-27',180),(4,1,'2023-09-10',210),
  (5,1,'2023-12-24',260),(6,1,'2024-01-07',290),
  (7,1,'2024-07-05',340),(8,1,'2024-08-20',380),(9,1,'2025-01-12',410),
  (10,2,'2023-02-15',60),(11,2,'2023-04-22',70),
  (12,2,'2023-08-30',95),(13,2,'2023-12-15',105),(14,2,'2024-02-20',95),
  (15,2,'2024-09-15',88),
  (16,3,'2024-04-04',300),(17,3,'2024-04-18',280),
  (18,3,'2024-07-27',260),
  (19,3,'2024-11-04',180),
  (20,3,'2025-02-22',150),
  (21,4,'2024-01-05',100),(22,4,'2024-02-15',100),
  (23,4,'2024-04-10',100),(24,4,'2024-06-22',100),(25,4,'2024-09-08',100),
  (26,4,'2024-11-22',100),(27,4,'2025-02-15',100);

WITH params AS (SELECT DATE '2025-04-01' AS snapshot),
flagged AS (
    SELECT o.customer_id, o.order_date, o.amount,
        CASE
          WHEN LAG(o.order_date) OVER w IS NULL THEN 1
          WHEN (o.order_date - LAG(o.order_date) OVER w) > 90 THEN 1
          ELSE 0
        END AS opens_island,
        ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - o.order_date) AS days_back
    FROM orders o
    WHERE o.order_date >= (SELECT snapshot FROM params) - INTERVAL '730 days'
    WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY o.customer_id ORDER BY o.order_date)
),
with_island AS (
    SELECT customer_id, order_date, amount, days_back, opens_island,
        SUM(opens_island) OVER (
            PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date
            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
        ) AS island_id
    FROM flagged
),
windowed AS (
    SELECT customer_id, order_date, amount, days_back, island_id,
        FIRST_VALUE(amount) OVER w_island AS first_amount,
        LAST_VALUE(amount)  OVER w_island AS last_amount
    FROM with_island
    WINDOW w_island AS (
        PARTITION BY customer_id, island_id
        ORDER BY order_date
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
    )
),
islands AS (
    SELECT customer_id, island_id,
        MIN(order_date) AS island_start, MAX(order_date) AS island_end,
        MAX(first_amount) AS first_amount, MAX(last_amount) AS last_amount
    FROM windowed
    GROUP BY customer_id, island_id
),
phase_pairs AS (
    SELECT customer_id, island_id,
        island_start AS comeback_date,
        first_amount AS comeback_aov,
        LAG(island_end)  OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY island_id) AS prev_island_end,
        LAG(last_amount) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY island_id) AS prev_island_aov,
        ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - island_start) AS days_back_comeback
    FROM islands
),
resurrections AS (
    SELECT customer_id, comeback_date, comeback_aov,
        prev_island_aov AS pre_churn_aov,
        (comeback_date - prev_island_end) AS gap_days,
        days_back_comeback
    FROM phase_pairs
    WHERE prev_island_end IS NOT NULL
      AND (comeback_date - prev_island_end) > 90
),
total_islands_per_cust AS (
    SELECT customer_id, MAX(island_id) AS total_islands
    FROM islands GROUP BY customer_id
),
per_customer AS (
    SELECT r.customer_id, ti.total_islands,
        COUNT(*) AS resurrection_cycles,
        ROUND(AVG(gap_days), 0) AS avg_gap_days,
        MAX(gap_days) AS max_gap_days,
        ROUND(AVG(pre_churn_aov), 2) AS avg_pre_churn_aov,
        ROUND(AVG(comeback_aov), 2) AS avg_comeback_aov,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back_comeback < 365 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS y1_resurrections,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back_comeback >= 365 AND days_back_comeback < 730 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS y2_resurrections,
        MAX(comeback_date) AS most_recent_comeback
    FROM resurrections r
    JOIN total_islands_per_cust ti ON ti.customer_id = r.customer_id
    GROUP BY r.customer_id, ti.total_islands
)
SELECT customer_id, total_islands, resurrection_cycles,
    avg_gap_days, max_gap_days, avg_pre_churn_aov, avg_comeback_aov,
    ROUND(avg_comeback_aov - avg_pre_churn_aov, 2) AS aov_uplift,
    y1_resurrections, y2_resurrections,
    CASE
        WHEN y2_resurrections = 0 AND y1_resurrections > 0 THEN 'y1_only'
        WHEN y1_resurrections > y2_resurrections THEN 'rising'
        WHEN y1_resurrections < y2_resurrections THEN 'falling'
        ELSE 'stable'
    END AS resurrection_trend,
    most_recent_comeback
FROM per_customer
ORDER BY resurrection_cycles DESC, aov_uplift DESC;

title: Return-to-Purchase Fraud — L7 / L28 / L90 Trending Detector
subtopic: L7/L28/L90 windows · trend vs baseline · burst detection
schema: orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status, l28_orders, l7_orders, l90_orders), returns(return_id, order_id, return_date, refund_amount, reason, customer_id, l28_returns, l7_returns, l90_returns), params(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status, l28_orders, l7_orders, l90_orders), return_pairs(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status, l28_orders, l7_orders, l90_orders)
scenario: A single 'lifetime return rate' number is too slow — by the time a customer's lifetime ratio crosses 40 % they've already cost the store thousands. Risk-Ops needs L7, L28, and L90 return rates so it can spot a recently-spiking customer before the cumulative number catches up. The flag fires when (a) L28 rate alone breaches 40 %, OR (b) L28 rate is 1.5× the L90 baseline (rising fast even if the absolute number is low), OR (c) any 7-day burst of more than 4 returns appears in the lifetime stream.
question:
Snapshot 2025-04-01. Per customer, return:
  customer_id, lifetime_orders, lifetime_returns, lifetime_return_rate,
  l7_orders, l7_returns, l7_return_rate,
  l28_orders, l28_returns, l28_return_rate,
  l90_orders, l90_returns, l90_return_rate,
  max_7d_return_burst    — most returns inside any 7-day rolling window,
  fraud_flag             — 'low_volume' if L90 orders < 5,
                           'burst' if max_7d_return_burst > 4,
                           'high_return_rate' if L28 rate > 0.40,
                           'rising_returns' if L28 rate > 1.5 × L90 rate,
                           else 'clean'.

Sort by fraud_flag priority (burst, high_return_rate, rising_returns,
low_volume, clean), then by l28_return_rate DESC.
solution:
WITH params AS (SELECT DATE '2025-04-01' AS snapshot),
o_e AS (
    SELECT o.*, ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - o.order_date::date) AS days_back
    FROM orders o
),
r_e AS (
    SELECT r.return_id, o.customer_id, r.return_date,
           ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - r.return_date::date) AS days_back
    FROM returns r
    JOIN orders o ON r.order_id = o.order_id
),
return_pairs AS (
    SELECT a.customer_id, a.return_date AS anchor,
           COUNT(*) AS burst_count
    FROM r_e a
    JOIN r_e b ON b.customer_id = a.customer_id
              AND b.return_date >= a.return_date
              AND (b.return_date::date - a.return_date::date) < 7
    GROUP BY a.customer_id, a.return_date
),
burst AS (
    SELECT customer_id, MAX(burst_count) AS max_7d_return_burst
    FROM return_pairs GROUP BY customer_id
),
order_w AS (
    SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS lifetime_orders,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l7_orders,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l28_orders,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 90 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l90_orders
    FROM o_e GROUP BY customer_id
),
return_w AS (
    SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS lifetime_returns,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l7_returns,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l28_returns,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 90 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l90_returns
    FROM r_e GROUP BY customer_id
),
joined AS (
    SELECT o.customer_id,
        o.lifetime_orders, COALESCE(r.lifetime_returns, 0) AS lifetime_returns,
        o.l7_orders, COALESCE(r.l7_returns, 0) AS l7_returns,
        o.l28_orders, COALESCE(r.l28_returns, 0) AS l28_returns,
        o.l90_orders, COALESCE(r.l90_returns, 0) AS l90_returns,
        COALESCE(b.max_7d_return_burst, 0) AS max_7d_return_burst
    FROM order_w o
    LEFT JOIN return_w r ON r.customer_id = o.customer_id
    LEFT JOIN burst b ON b.customer_id = o.customer_id
)
SELECT customer_id, lifetime_orders, lifetime_returns,
    ROUND(1.0*lifetime_returns/NULLIF(lifetime_orders,0), 2) AS lifetime_return_rate,
    l7_orders, l7_returns,
    ROUND(1.0*l7_returns/NULLIF(l7_orders,0), 2) AS l7_return_rate,
    l28_orders, l28_returns,
    ROUND(1.0*l28_returns/NULLIF(l28_orders,0), 2) AS l28_return_rate,
    l90_orders, l90_returns,
    ROUND(1.0*l90_returns/NULLIF(l90_orders,0), 2) AS l90_return_rate,
    max_7d_return_burst,
    CASE
        WHEN l90_orders < 5 THEN 'low_volume'
        WHEN max_7d_return_burst > 4 THEN 'burst'
        WHEN 1.0*l28_returns/NULLIF(l28_orders,0) > 0.40 THEN 'high_return_rate'
        WHEN 1.0*l28_returns/NULLIF(l28_orders,0)
             > 1.5*COALESCE(1.0*l90_returns/NULLIF(l90_orders,0), 0)
             AND l28_returns >= 2 THEN 'rising_returns'
        ELSE 'clean'
    END AS fraud_flag
FROM joined
ORDER BY
    CASE
      WHEN max_7d_return_burst > 4 THEN 1
      WHEN 1.0*l28_returns/NULLIF(l28_orders,0) > 0.40 THEN 2
      WHEN 1.0*l28_returns/NULLIF(l28_orders,0)
           > 1.5*COALESCE(1.0*l90_returns/NULLIF(l90_orders,0),0)
           AND l28_returns >= 2 THEN 3
      WHEN l90_orders < 5 THEN 4
      ELSE 5
    END,
    l28_return_rate DESC;

title: Fraud Signal — More Than 3 Shipping Addresses In a 30-Day Window
schema: orders(order_id, customer_id, order_ts, amount, shipping_address, order_date, status)
scenario: A common account-takeover or reshipping-mule fraud pattern is a single account shipping orders to many distinct physical addresses in a short timeframe. Risk wants a daily report of every customer who used MORE than 3 distinct shipping addresses inside any rolling 30-day window — a hard cutoff that goes straight to manual review.
question:
Return one row per offending customer:
  customer_id | window_start | window_end | distinct_addresses | total_amount

where the window is the first 30-day stretch in which the customer's distinct shipping-address count exceeds 3 (i.e. ≥ 4 distinct addresses). Order by distinct_addresses DESC, then by customer_id ASC.
solution:
-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Setup — TIMESTAMP-typed order_ts so the INTERVAL window  │
-- │  arithmetic runs natively in PostgreSQL.                  │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER,
    order_ts TIMESTAMP, amount NUMERIC, shipping_address TEXT
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  -- Customer 1: 4 different addresses, all within 30 days → fraud signal
  (1, 1, '2025-04-01 09:00:00', 200, '1 Pine St'),
  (2, 1, '2025-04-05 12:30:00', 350, '7 Birch Ln'),
  (3, 1, '2025-04-12 18:45:00', 180, '22 Cedar Rd'),
  (4, 1, '2025-04-19 02:55:00', 410, '88 Elm Way'),
  -- Customer 2: 1 address only — not flagged
  (5, 2, '2025-04-01 09:00:00', 150, '12 Oak Dr'),
  (6, 2, '2025-04-05 14:00:00', 180, '12 Oak Dr'),
  (7, 2, '2025-04-10 19:00:00', 220, '12 Oak Dr'),
  (8, 2, '2025-04-15 09:00:00', 100, '12 Oak Dr'),
  (9, 2, '2025-04-20 09:00:00', 100, '12 Oak Dr'),
  -- Customer 3: 4 addresses but spread over 5 months — not flagged
  (10, 3, '2025-01-01 09:00:00', 120, '5 Maple Ct'),
  (11, 3, '2025-02-15 14:00:00', 130, '9 Walnut Ave'),
  (12, 3, '2025-04-04 19:00:00', 140, '11 Spruce Pl'),
  (13, 3, '2025-05-30 19:00:00', 150, '13 Aspen Way'),
  -- Customer 4: 3 addresses in 25 days — at threshold, just under the > 3 rule
  (14, 4, '2025-04-01 09:00:00', 90, 'A1'),
  (15, 4, '2025-04-10 18:00:00', 90, 'A2'),
  (16, 4, '2025-04-25 08:00:00', 90, 'A3'),
  -- Customer 5: 5 addresses in 25 days → fraud signal
  (17, 5, '2025-03-05 09:00:00', 50, 'X1'),
  (18, 5, '2025-03-10 09:00:00', 50, 'X2'),
  (19, 5, '2025-03-15 09:00:00', 50, 'X3'),
  (20, 5, '2025-03-20 09:00:00', 50, 'X4'),
  (21, 5, '2025-03-30 09:00:00', 50, 'X5');

-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Solution — collapse to (customer, address) first-seen,   │
-- │  then self-join for 30-day windows. Native PG INTERVAL    │
-- │  replaces SQLite's datetime(ts, '+30 days').              │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH first_addr AS (
    SELECT customer_id, shipping_address,
           MIN(order_ts) AS first_ts,
           SUM(amount)   AS addr_spend
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY customer_id, shipping_address
),
windowed AS (
    SELECT a.customer_id,
           a.first_ts                 AS window_start,
           MAX(b.first_ts)            AS window_end,
           COUNT(*)                   AS distinct_addresses,
           SUM(b.addr_spend)          AS total_amount
    FROM first_addr a
    JOIN first_addr b
      ON b.customer_id = a.customer_id
     AND b.first_ts BETWEEN a.first_ts
                        AND a.first_ts + INTERVAL '30 days'
    GROUP BY a.customer_id, a.first_ts
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 3
),
ranked AS (
    SELECT windowed.*,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id
                              ORDER BY distinct_addresses DESC,
                                       window_start ASC) AS rn
    FROM windowed
)
SELECT customer_id, window_start, window_end,
       distinct_addresses, total_amount
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY distinct_addresses DESC, customer_id ASC;

title: Passive Customer Engagement Velocity — L7 vs L28 Daily Rate
subtopic: L7/L28/L90 sessions · daily-rate trend · cart velocity
schema: sessions(session_id, customer_id, session_date, added_to_cart, checked_out), orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status), params(customer_id, signup_date)
scenario: Marketing's 'passive' bucket is too coarse — a window-shopper who's been browsing more this week than last week is a hot lead, while one whose visits are tailing off is going cold. The team wants a daily report that compares each non-buying customer's L7 daily session rate to their L28 daily rate (a velocity signal), and labels them warming / cooling / stable / cold. The L7 / L28 cart-add ratios go on the same row so the team can see whether a heating-up customer is also leaning on the cart.
question:
Snapshot 2025-04-01. For each customer with at least 5 lifetime sessions and ZERO orders, return:
  customer_id, lifetime_sessions, lifetime_cart_adds,
  l7_sessions, l28_sessions, l90_sessions,
  l7_cart_adds, l28_cart_adds,
  l7_daily_sessions, l28_daily_sessions,    -- per-day rates
  cart_velocity   = l7_daily_sessions / NULLIF(l28_daily_sessions, 0),
  engagement_trend ∈ {'warming','cooling','stable','cold'}

  • 'cold'    if l28_sessions = 0
  • 'warming' if l7_daily_sessions > 1.5 × l28_daily_sessions
  • 'cooling' if l7_daily_sessions < 0.5 × l28_daily_sessions
  • else 'stable'

Sort by 'warming' first, then by l28_sessions DESC.
solution:
WITH params AS (SELECT DATE '2025-04-01' AS snapshot),
sess AS (
    SELECT s.session_id, s.customer_id, s.session_date,
           CASE WHEN s.added_to_cart::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' THEN s.added_to_cart::numeric ELSE 0 END AS added_to_cart,
           ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - s.session_date::date) AS days_back
    FROM sessions s
)
SELECT s.customer_id,
    COUNT(*) AS lifetime_sessions,
    SUM(s.added_to_cart) AS lifetime_cart_adds,
    SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l7_sessions,
    SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l28_sessions,
    SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 90 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l90_sessions,
    SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN s.added_to_cart ELSE 0 END) AS l7_cart_adds,
    SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN s.added_to_cart ELSE 0 END) AS l28_cart_adds,
    ROUND(1.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / 7.0, 2) AS l7_daily_sessions,
    ROUND(1.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / 28.0, 2) AS l28_daily_sessions,
    ROUND(
        (1.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / 7.0)
        / NULLIF(1.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / 28.0, 0),
    2) AS cart_velocity,
    CASE
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) = 0 THEN 'cold'
        WHEN (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/7.0)
           > 1.5 * (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/28.0) THEN 'warming'
        WHEN (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/7.0)
           < 0.5 * (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/28.0) THEN 'cooling'
        ELSE 'stable'
    END AS engagement_trend
FROM sess s
WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = s.customer_id)
GROUP BY s.customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5
ORDER BY
    CASE
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) = 0 THEN 4
        WHEN (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/7.0)
           > 1.5 * (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/28.0) THEN 1
        WHEN (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/7.0)
           < 0.5 * (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/28.0) THEN 3
        ELSE 2
    END,
    l28_sessions DESC;

title: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) — pandas
solution:
import pandas as pd


def customer_lifetime_value(orders: pd.DataFrame,
                            gross_margin: float = 0.25,
                            discount_rate: float = 0.10) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Discounted-Cash-Flow Customer Lifetime Value per customer.

    The function tells you how much profit each customer has actually
    generated, but penalises profit earned in later years to honour the
    time value of money. CFOs use this output to check whether Customer
    Acquisition Cost is sustainable — if average CLV is $50 but ad spend
    to acquire one customer is $60, the business is leaking money even
    when the top-line sales chart looks healthy.

    Inputs
        orders columns: customer_id, order_date (YYYY-MM-DD), amount, status.
        gross_margin   — fraction of revenue retained as profit (e.g. 0.25).
        discount_rate  — annual cost of capital (e.g. 0.10 = 10 %).

    Output
        One row per customer: customer_id, first_order_date, last_order_date,
        orders, total_spend, gross_margin, clv (ordered by clv DESC).

    Pro-tip
        A wide gap between gross_margin and clv means customers are
        spreading their spend over many years; a healthy book has the two
        numbers close together, signalling early, decisive spend.
    """
    # 1. Setup — only realised revenue counts. .copy() keeps callers safe
    #    and pd.to_datetime makes year arithmetic possible downstream.
    paid = orders[orders['status'] == 'paid'].copy()
    paid['order_date'] = pd.to_datetime(paid['order_date'])
    paid['margin'] = paid['amount'] * gross_margin

    # 2. Time-based discounting — DCF: margin / (1 + r) ** t  where t
    #    is the calendar-year offset from each customer's FIRST paid
    #    order. groupby(...).transform('min') keeps the result the same
    #    length as `paid` so we can subtract column-wise without a join.
    first = paid.groupby('customer_id')['order_date'].transform('min')
    paid['year_offset'] = (paid['order_date'].dt.year - first.dt.year)
    paid['discounted'] = paid['margin'] / (1 + discount_rate) ** paid['year_offset']

    # 3. Aggregate to one row per customer — the CFO summary.
    out = (
        paid.groupby('customer_id')
            .agg(first_order_date=('order_date', 'min'),
                 last_order_date=('order_date', 'max'),
                 orders=('amount', 'size'),
                 total_spend=('amount', 'sum'),
                 gross_margin=('margin', 'sum'),
                 clv=('discounted', 'sum'))
            .reset_index()
    )

    # 4. Round + sort: highest-value customers (the "whales") on top.
    out['clv'] = out['clv'].round(2)
    out['gross_margin'] = out['gross_margin'].round(2)
    return out.sort_values('clv', ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)

title: Unsteady Passive Customers — High Cart-Add, High Abandonment
solution:
import pandas as pd


def unsteady_passive(events: pd.DataFrame, days: int = 30) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Identify "unsteady" passive customers — they fill the cart but rarely
    check out. They sit between a window-shopper (no cart adds at all) and
    a buyer (purchases). They're the cheapest segment to convert: a free-
    shipping or first-purchase coupon often closes the gap.

    Inputs
        events columns: customer_id, event_ts (datetime), event_type
        ∈ {'view', 'add_to_cart', 'checkout', 'purchase'}.
        days — lookback window from the most recent event in the data.

    Output
        customer_id | cart_adds | checkouts | purchases |
        abandonment_rate | label
        label ∈ {'unsteady','window_shopper','checkout_dropout','buyer','browser'}.

    Pro-tip
        Abandonment rate alone is noisy on a single cart-add; the
        cart_adds >= 3 floor filters out random one-off adds and keeps
        the cohort marketable.
    """
    # 1. Window the recent activity using the latest event as the anchor —
    #    works for both live data and historical replay tests.
    events = events.copy()
    events['event_ts'] = pd.to_datetime(events['event_ts'])
    cutoff = events['event_ts'].max() - pd.Timedelta(days=days)
    recent = events[events['event_ts'] >= cutoff]

    # 2. Long → wide pivot. Each event_type becomes a column whose value
    #    is the per-customer count; fill_value=0 covers customers who
    #    never reached, e.g., 'purchase'.
    pivot = (
        recent.assign(n=1)
              .pivot_table(index='customer_id', columns='event_type',
                           values='n', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)
              .reset_index()
    )
    for col in ('view', 'add_to_cart', 'checkout', 'purchase'):
        if col not in pivot.columns:
            pivot[col] = 0

    # 3. Abandonment rate = 1 - conversion. Replace 0 cart-adds with NA
    #    so we don't divide by zero, then clip into [0, 1].
    pivot['abandonment_rate'] = (
        1 - pivot['purchase'] / pivot['add_to_cart'].replace(0, pd.NA)
    ).fillna(0).clip(lower=0).round(2)

    # 4. Label rules — order matters: 'unsteady' wins over plain
    #    'cart_abandoner' because it's the actionable cohort.
    def _label(r):
        if r['add_to_cart'] >= 3 and r['abandonment_rate'] >= 0.6:
            return 'unsteady'
        if r['purchase'] > 0:
            return 'buyer'
        if r['checkout'] > 0:
            return 'checkout_dropout'
        if r['add_to_cart'] == 0 and r['purchase'] == 0:
            return 'window_shopper'
        return 'browser'
    pivot['label'] = pivot.apply(_label, axis=1)

    # 5. Rename to friendlier columns + sort the highest-abandonment
    #    customers to the top so marketing sees them first.
    out = pivot.rename(columns={'add_to_cart': 'cart_adds',
                                'checkout':    'checkouts',
                                'purchase':    'purchases'})
    return (out[['customer_id','cart_adds','checkouts','purchases',
                 'abandonment_rate','label']]
            .sort_values(['abandonment_rate','cart_adds'],
                         ascending=[False, False])
            .reset_index(drop=True))

title: Velocity-Rule Fraud Detection — Sliding Window in pandas
solution:
import pandas as pd


def velocity_alerts(orders: pd.DataFrame,
                    window_minutes: int = 10,
                    max_orders: int = 4,
                    max_amount: float = 2_000.0) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Flag customers whose order rate breaks a velocity threshold inside a
    rolling time window. This is the most common batch fraud-detection
    signal: a normal customer rarely places 5 orders in 10 minutes; a
    stolen-card abuser does.

    Inputs
        orders columns: order_id, customer_id, order_ts, amount.
        window_minutes — width of the rolling look-back window.
        max_orders     — orders-per-window threshold; > triggers alert.
        max_amount     — currency-per-window threshold; > triggers alert.

    Output
        customer_id | trigger_ts | orders_in_window | amount_in_window | rule
        rule ∈ {'too_many_orders', 'high_amount', 'both'}.

    Pro-tip
        Pandas' time-offset rolling needs a sorted DatetimeIndex —
        the per-customer sort below isn't optional, it's what unlocks
        the optimised offset-based path. Skip the sort and you fall
        back to a slow, full-scan rolling.
    """
    # 1. Normalise + sort. Rolling on a time-offset window requires a
    #    monotonic DatetimeIndex per group.
    df = orders.copy()
    df['order_ts'] = pd.to_datetime(df['order_ts'])
    df = df.sort_values(['customer_id', 'order_ts'])

    pieces = []
    win = pd.Timedelta(minutes=window_minutes)

    # 2. Per-customer rolling. We use a time-offset window ('10min') so
    #    the look-back is wall-clock, not row-count — six orders in 30
    #    seconds and one order an hour later don't count as one window.
    for cust, g in df.groupby('customer_id'):
        g = g.set_index('order_ts')
        cnt = g['order_id'].rolling(win).count()
        amt = g['amount'].rolling(win).sum()

        # 3. A row is "bad" if either threshold breaks. Emit the FIRST
        #    such row only — alert fatigue is a real on-call problem.
        bad = (cnt > max_orders) | (amt > max_amount)
        if not bad.any():
            continue
        first_idx = bad.idxmax()
        n = int(cnt.loc[first_idx])
        a = float(amt.loc[first_idx])

        # 4. Tag which rule fired so the analyst doesn't recompute the
        #    threshold check from the raw numbers.
        if n > max_orders and a > max_amount:
            rule = 'both'
        elif n > max_orders:
            rule = 'too_many_orders'
        else:
            rule = 'high_amount'
        pieces.append({
            'customer_id': cust,
            'trigger_ts': first_idx,
            'orders_in_window': n,
            'amount_in_window': round(a, 2),
            'rule': rule,
        })

    # 5. Earliest alerts to the top so the queue is FIFO.
    return (pd.DataFrame(pieces)
              .sort_values('trigger_ts')
              .reset_index(drop=True))


# ─── Runnable demo with seeded data ───────────────────────────────────
if __name__ == "__main__":
    import pandas as pd

    # Seeded orders — customer 7 is a card-tester (5 orders in 4 min, all in 9:00-9:04);
    # customer 11 is a high-amount whale (one big-ticket buy);
    # customer 3 is normal traffic (1-2 orders/hour spread out).
    orders = pd.DataFrame([
        # Card-tester: 5 orders in 4 minutes — should trip too_many_orders
        {"order_id": 101, "customer_id": 7,  "order_ts": "2024-04-01 09:00:00", "amount":   42.0},
        {"order_id": 102, "customer_id": 7,  "order_ts": "2024-04-01 09:01:00", "amount":   45.0},
        {"order_id": 103, "customer_id": 7,  "order_ts": "2024-04-01 09:02:00", "amount":   38.0},
        {"order_id": 104, "customer_id": 7,  "order_ts": "2024-04-01 09:03:00", "amount":   41.0},
        {"order_id": 105, "customer_id": 7,  "order_ts": "2024-04-01 09:04:00", "amount":   47.0},
        # High-amount whale: one $2500 charge — trips high_amount
        {"order_id": 201, "customer_id": 11, "order_ts": "2024-04-01 10:15:00", "amount": 2500.0},
        # Normal customer: spread-out orders, well under both thresholds
        {"order_id": 301, "customer_id": 3,  "order_ts": "2024-04-01 09:00:00", "amount":  120.0},
        {"order_id": 302, "customer_id": 3,  "order_ts": "2024-04-01 10:00:00", "amount":   80.0},
        {"order_id": 303, "customer_id": 3,  "order_ts": "2024-04-01 11:30:00", "amount":  110.0},
    ])

    alerts = velocity_alerts(orders, window_minutes=10,
                              max_orders=4, max_amount=2000.0)

    print("=== INPUT — 9 orders across 3 customers ===")
    print(orders.to_string(index=False))
    print()
    print("=== VELOCITY ALERTS ===")
    if alerts.empty:
        print("(no alerts)")
    else:
        print(alerts.to_string(index=False))
    print()
    print(f"Total customers in input:  {orders['customer_id'].nunique()}")
    print(f"Customers flagged:         {alerts['customer_id'].nunique() if not alerts.empty else 0}")

title: Quick Ratio of Customer Growth — Monthly Health Score
solution:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd


def quick_ratio(monthly: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Customer-base health score per month using the Quick Ratio:
        QR = (new + resurrected) / churned
    QR > 1 means the store is acquiring + reviving faster than it's losing
    customers. QR < 1 is net churn — the wrong direction even when raw
    signups look fine on the marketing dashboard.

    Inputs
        monthly columns: year_month (YYYY-MM), bucket, customers
        bucket ∈ {'new', 'resurrected', 'existing', 'churned'}.

    Output
        year_month | new | resurrected | existing | churned |
        net_change | quick_ratio | health
        health ∈ {'growing', 'flat', 'declining'}.

    Pro-tip
        A month with churned = 0 returns +∞ — that's correct in the
        framework but blows up downstream charts. Coerce to a finite
        ceiling (e.g. 99) before plotting if your dashboard library
        can't handle inf.
    """
    # 1. Long → wide pivot so every (year_month, bucket) pair becomes
    #    one column. fill_value=0 covers months where a bucket was empty.
    wide = monthly.pivot_table(index='year_month',
                               columns='bucket',
                               values='customers',
                               aggfunc='sum',
                               fill_value=0).reset_index()
    for col in ('new', 'resurrected', 'existing', 'churned'):
        if col not in wide.columns:
            wide[col] = 0

    # 2. Net change + Quick Ratio. NULLIF-style replace avoids the
    #    divide-by-zero; we treat zero churn as +∞ (perfectly healthy).
    wide['net_change'] = wide['new'] + wide['resurrected'] - wide['churned']
    denom = wide['churned'].replace(0, np.nan)
    wide['quick_ratio'] = ((wide['new'] + wide['resurrected']) / denom).round(2)
    wide['quick_ratio'] = wide['quick_ratio'].fillna(np.inf)

    # 3. Health label. The 0.8 / 1.2 bands absorb single-month noise;
    #    don't react to a 0.95 → 1.05 swing on a small base.
    def _health(r):
        if r['churned'] == 0 and (r['new'] + r['resurrected']) > 0:
            return 'growing'
        if r['quick_ratio'] > 1.2:
            return 'growing'
        if r['quick_ratio'] < 0.8:
            return 'declining'
        return 'flat'
    wide['health'] = wide.apply(_health, axis=1)

    # 4. Time-ordered output for trend charts.
    return (wide[['year_month','new','resurrected','existing',
                  'churned','net_change','quick_ratio','health']]
            .sort_values('year_month').reset_index(drop=True))

title: PyFlink Streaming — Tumbling-Window Order Velocity Fraud Detector
solution:
"""
PyFlink streaming job — flag a customer whenever they exceed either
velocity threshold inside a 1-minute tumbling event-time window:
    • > 4 orders in the window, OR
    • > $2,000 spent in the window
The detector emits ONE alert per offending window so a downstream fraud
queue gets a deduplicated firehose, not one alert per order.

Why event-time, not processing-time
    Orders arrive late (mobile clients, network jitter). A processing-
    time window would put a 11:59 order placed at 12:01 into the WRONG
    window. WatermarkStrategy.for_bounded_out_of_orderness waits 5 s
    for stragglers, then closes the window deterministically.

Pro-tip
    TumblingEventTimeWindows is hard-aligned to the wall clock
    (00 s – 60 s). Two fast-fingered customers placing 3 orders each
    in a 30-second burst that straddles the minute boundary will NOT
    trigger because counts reset at the minute. If you need
    overlapping coverage, switch to SlidingEventTimeWindows or HOP in
    the Table API (see q18).
"""
from pyflink.common import Time, Types, WatermarkStrategy, Duration
from pyflink.common.watermark_strategy import TimestampAssigner
from pyflink.datastream import StreamExecutionEnvironment
from pyflink.datastream.functions import ProcessWindowFunction
from pyflink.datastream.window import TumblingEventTimeWindows


class _OrderTimestamp(TimestampAssigner):
    """Pull event_ts_ms from the order tuple — Flink wants ms epochs."""
    def extract_timestamp(self, value, record_timestamp):
        return int(value[3])


class VelocityAlert(ProcessWindowFunction):
    """For one (customer_id, window) the function sees ALL events in the
    window. Compute count + total once and emit at most one row — Flink
    dedupes by key + window automatically."""
    def process(self, customer_id, ctx, elements):
        # 1. Materialise the iterable so we can count and sum in one pass.
        rows = list(elements)
        n = len(rows)
        total = sum(r[2] for r in rows)

        # 2. Apply both thresholds. Tag the rule that fired so the
        #    on-call doesn't have to recompute the check.
        if n > 4 or total > 2_000.0:
            rule = ('both' if n > 4 and total > 2_000.0
                    else 'too_many_orders' if n > 4
                    else 'high_amount')
            yield (customer_id, ctx.window().start, n, float(total), rule)


def build_pipeline(env, source):
    """Wire watermarks → key_by → window → process. The caller owns the
    source so the same code runs against Kafka and an in-memory unit
    test, just by swapping the source argument."""
    # 1. Watermarks let the job tolerate small (≤ 5 s) lateness without
    #    waiting forever for stragglers.
    watermarks = (
        WatermarkStrategy
        .for_bounded_out_of_orderness(Duration.of_seconds(5))
        .with_timestamp_assigner(_OrderTimestamp())
    )

    # 2. Key-by-customer scopes the window so each customer's events
    #    are bucketed independently — a sequential process per key.
    alerts = (
        source.assign_timestamps_and_watermarks(watermarks)
              .key_by(lambda r: r[1])  # customer_id
              .window(TumblingEventTimeWindows.of(Time.minutes(1)))
              .process(VelocityAlert(),
                       output_type=Types.TUPLE([
                           Types.LONG(), Types.LONG(),
                           Types.INT(),  Types.DOUBLE(),
                           Types.STRING()]))
    )
    alerts.print('FRAUD_ALERT')
    return alerts


if __name__ == '__main__':
    env = StreamExecutionEnvironment.get_execution_environment()
    env.set_parallelism(2)
    sample = env.from_collection(
        collection=[
            (1, 101, 600.0, 1_700_000_000_000),
            (2, 101, 700.0, 1_700_000_010_000),
            (3, 101, 800.0, 1_700_000_020_000),  # 3 orders, $2_100 → high_amount
            (4, 102, 100.0, 1_700_000_030_000),
        ],
        type_info=Types.TUPLE([Types.LONG(), Types.LONG(),
                                Types.DOUBLE(), Types.LONG()]))
    build_pipeline(env, sample)
    env.execute('velocity-fraud-detector')

title: PyFlink Streaming — Address-Change Followed By High-Value Order
solution:
"""
A common account-takeover pattern: the attacker quietly updates the
shipping address, then within 30 minutes places an unusually large
order. We detect that two-step pattern with a KeyedProcessFunction
that holds a tiny ValueState per customer — last_address +
addr_changed_at — and emits an alert when both conditions hold.

Why ValueState and not a window
    The two events (address_change and order) are sparse and don't
    align to any natural window boundary. Keyed state is the right
    primitive: O(1) per event, persisted across restarts via Flink's
    checkpoint/savepoint machinery, and naturally scoped per customer.

Pro-tip
    The 30-minute / $1,000 thresholds are the policy knobs you'll
    end up tuning in production. Wire them to a config service
    rather than hard-coding them here so risk-ops can adjust without
    a code deploy + cluster restart.
"""
from pyflink.common import Types, WatermarkStrategy, Duration
from pyflink.common.state import ValueStateDescriptor
from pyflink.common.watermark_strategy import TimestampAssigner
from pyflink.datastream import StreamExecutionEnvironment
from pyflink.datastream.functions import KeyedProcessFunction

HIGH_VALUE = 1_000.0
WINDOW_MS  = 30 * 60 * 1000  # 30 minutes


class _EventTs(TimestampAssigner):
    def extract_timestamp(self, value, record_timestamp):
        return int(value['ts'])


class AddressChangeThenBigOrder(KeyedProcessFunction):
    """State per customer: { last_address, changed_at }."""

    def open(self, ctx):
        # 1. Register the keyed state. PICKLED_BYTE_ARRAY lets us store
        #    a small Python dict; the alternative is two MapState/
        #    ValueState slots, but for two scalar fields the dict is
        #    simpler and the savepoint footprint is identical.
        self.state = ctx.get_state(
            ValueStateDescriptor('addr_state', Types.PICKLED_BYTE_ARRAY()))

    def process_element(self, ev, ctx):
        st = self.state.value() or {'last_address': None, 'changed_at': None}

        # 2. Address change → update the watermark in state. Only
        #    record the change_at when the address actually differs;
        #    a redundant "set same address" shouldn't reset the timer.
        if ev['type'] == 'address_change':
            if st['last_address'] and ev['address'] != st['last_address']:
                st['changed_at'] = ev['ts']
            st['last_address'] = ev['address']
            self.state.update(st)

        # 3. Order → check the two-step pattern: address change in the
        #    last 30 min AND order >= $1,000. Both must hold.
        elif ev['type'] == 'order':
            ca = st.get('changed_at')
            if ca and (ev['ts'] - ca) <= WINDOW_MS and ev['amount'] >= HIGH_VALUE:
                yield {
                    'customer_id': ev['customer_id'],
                    'order_id':    ev['order_id'],
                    'amount':      ev['amount'],
                    'gap_minutes': round((ev['ts'] - ca) / 60_000, 1),
                    'reason':      'addr_change_then_high_value',
                }


def build_pipeline(env, source):
    """Caller-owned source — same job runs against Kafka or unit tests."""
    watermarks = (
        WatermarkStrategy
        .for_bounded_out_of_orderness(Duration.of_seconds(5))
        .with_timestamp_assigner(_EventTs())
    )
    return (
        source.assign_timestamps_and_watermarks(watermarks)
              .key_by(lambda e: e['customer_id'])
              .process(AddressChangeThenBigOrder(),
                       output_type=Types.PICKLED_BYTE_ARRAY())
    )

title: Tiered Re-engagement Coupon Allocator — Incentive Campaign
solution:
import pandas as pd


TIER_DISCOUNT = {'Tier 1': 0.20, 'Tier 2': 0.10, 'Tier 3': 0.05}
STATE_BUMP    = {'resurrected': 0.05, 'passive': 0.10, 'churned': 0.15}
MAX_DISCOUNT  = 0.30


def allocate_coupons(customers: pd.DataFrame,
                     fraud_excluded_ids: set | None = None) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Build the next coupon-drop list — a per-customer discount derived from
    BOTH their loyalty tier (a static signal) and their lifecycle state
    (a dynamic signal). The 30 % cap protects gross margin from
    compounding bumps stacking on top of an already-rich tier discount.

    Why two-signal stacking
        Tier-only drops over-reward the customers who would buy anyway
        and miss the resurrection lever. State-only drops under-reward
        a Tier 1 churn (the one you really want back). Combining them
        solves both, and the cap prevents a "Tier 1 churned" from
        getting 35 % off the house.

    Inputs
        customers columns: customer_id, tier, lifecycle_state, last_spend.
        fraud_excluded_ids: optional set of ids to skip outright.

    Output
        customer_id | tier | lifecycle_state | discount_pct |
        expected_redemption | suggested_coupon_code

    Pro-tip
        expected_redemption = last_spend × discount_pct is the cleanest
        per-coupon cost estimate. The marketing CFO will want it before
        approving the campaign budget.
    """
    # 1. Hard-exclude fraud-flagged customers — they don't get coupons,
    #    period. The set lookup is O(1).
    excl = fraud_excluded_ids or set()
    df = customers[~customers['customer_id'].isin(excl)].copy()

    # 2. Stack tier base discount + lifecycle bump, then clip at the cap.
    #    .map(...).fillna(0) silently treats unknown tiers/states as 0
    #    instead of raising — robust to schema drift.
    df['discount_pct'] = (
        df['tier'].map(TIER_DISCOUNT).fillna(0)
        + df['lifecycle_state'].map(STATE_BUMP).fillna(0)
    ).clip(upper=MAX_DISCOUNT)

    # 3. Expected redemption — a back-of-envelope cost-per-coupon used
    #    by the marketing CFO to size the campaign budget.
    df['expected_redemption'] = (df['last_spend'] * df['discount_pct']).round(2)

    # 4. Build a human-readable coupon code (so support can audit it
    #    and customers feel like they got something tier-specific).
    df['suggested_coupon_code'] = (
        df['tier'].str.replace(' ', '').str.upper() + '-'
        + df['lifecycle_state'].str.upper().str[:3] + '-'
        + (df['discount_pct'] * 100).astype(int).astype(str) + 'OFF'
    )

    # 5. Highest-cost coupons first so the CFO can sanity-check the
    #    most expensive drops at a glance.
    return (df[['customer_id','tier','lifecycle_state','discount_pct',
                'expected_redemption','suggested_coupon_code']]
            .sort_values('expected_redemption', ascending=False)
            .reset_index(drop=True))

title: Fraud-Aware Incentive Eligibility — Severity-Weighted L7/L28/L90 Risk Score
subtopic: Severity weights · L7/L28/L90 burst · campaign eligibility
schema: customers(customer_id, lifecycle_state, tier, name, email, signup_date, country, city), fraud_signals(customer_id, signal_ts, signal_type, severity), campaigns(campaign_id, name, target_state, min_tier), params(customer_id, lifecycle_state, tier)
scenario: Hard binary fraud flags miss the customer with three LOW signals in the last week — individually they don't escalate, but the burst is the same fingerprint as a synthetic-account abuser. Risk-Ops wants a numeric risk score (severity-weighted by recency) plus L7 / L28 / L90 signal counts AND the customer's longest streak of consecutive-day signals (gaps-and-islands on the signal stream). The eligibility decision is decision-grade, not yes/no: 'hard_excluded' if any HIGH signal in the last 90 days OR risk_score ≥ 15 OR longest_burst ≥ 5; 'manual_review' if any MEDIUM signal in the last 30 days OR risk_score 5–14 OR longest_burst ≥ 3; otherwise 'eligible'.
question:
Given the campaign with name = 'win_back_q2_2025' and snapshot 2025-05-01, return:
  customer_id, tier, lifecycle_state,
  l7_signals, l28_signals, l90_signals,
  high_l90, medium_l30, low_l28,
  longest_burst   — longest run of consecutive calendar days
                    with at least one signal (gaps-and-islands).
  risk_score   = (HIGH within 90d × 10) + (MEDIUM within 30d × 5)
               + (LOW within 28d × 1),
  status       'hard_excluded' / 'manual_review' / 'eligible'

Hard-excluded customers ARE returned (risk-ops audits the queue). Filter to customers whose tier ranks at or above the campaign's min_tier and whose lifecycle_state matches target_state. Sort by risk_score DESC, customer_id ASC.
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS customers;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS fraud_signals;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS campaigns;
CREATE TABLE customers (
    customer_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    lifecycle_state TEXT, tier TEXT
);
CREATE TABLE fraud_signals (
    customer_id INTEGER, signal_ts DATE,
    signal_type TEXT, severity TEXT
);
CREATE TABLE campaigns (
    campaign_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT, target_state TEXT, min_tier TEXT
);
INSERT INTO customers VALUES
  (1,'churned','Tier 1'),(2,'churned','Tier 1'),(3,'churned','Tier 2'),
  (4,'churned','Tier 1'),(5,'churned','Tier 2'),(6,'active','Tier 1'),
  (7,'churned','Tier 3'),(8,'churned','Tier 2');
INSERT INTO fraud_signals VALUES
  (2,'2025-03-02','address_change_burst','HIGH'),
  (3,'2025-04-12','high_return_rate','MEDIUM'),
  (4,'2025-04-08','velocity','LOW'),(4,'2025-04-15','velocity','LOW'),
  (4,'2025-04-22','velocity','LOW'),(4,'2025-04-28','velocity','LOW'),
  (4,'2025-03-26','velocity','MEDIUM'),
  (5,'2025-04-04','velocity','LOW'),(5,'2025-04-08','velocity','LOW'),
  (5,'2025-04-12','velocity','LOW'),(5,'2025-04-16','velocity','LOW'),
  (5,'2025-04-20','velocity','LOW'),(5,'2025-04-24','velocity','LOW'),
  (5,'2025-04-28','velocity','LOW'),(5,'2025-04-06','high_return_rate','MEDIUM'),
  (5,'2025-04-09','address_change_burst','MEDIUM'),
  (8,'2024-09-10','velocity','LOW');
INSERT INTO campaigns VALUES (1,'win_back_q2_2025','churned','Tier 2');

WITH params AS (SELECT DATE '2025-05-01' AS snapshot),
cmp AS (SELECT * FROM campaigns WHERE name = 'win_back_q2_2025'),
sig AS (
    SELECT f.customer_id, f.severity, f.signal_ts,
           ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - f.signal_ts) AS days_back
    FROM fraud_signals f
    WHERE f.signal_ts >= (SELECT snapshot FROM params) - INTERVAL '90 days'
),
sig_days AS (
    SELECT customer_id, signal_ts AS signal_day
    FROM sig
    GROUP BY customer_id, signal_ts
),
sig_indexed AS (
    SELECT customer_id, signal_day,
           (signal_day - DATE '2000-01-01')
             - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY signal_day) AS island_id
    FROM sig_days
),
sig_islands AS (
    SELECT customer_id, island_id, COUNT(*) AS streak_len
    FROM sig_indexed GROUP BY customer_id, island_id
),
burst_per_cust AS (
    SELECT customer_id, MAX(streak_len) AS longest_burst
    FROM sig_islands GROUP BY customer_id
),
fraud_summary AS (
    SELECT customer_id,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l7_signals,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l28_signals,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 90 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l90_signals,
        SUM(CASE WHEN severity = 'HIGH' AND days_back <= 90 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS high_l90,
        SUM(CASE WHEN severity = 'MEDIUM' AND days_back <= 30 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS medium_l30,
        SUM(CASE WHEN severity = 'LOW' AND days_back <= 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS low_l28,
        SUM(CASE WHEN severity='HIGH' AND days_back <= 90 THEN 10
                 WHEN severity='MEDIUM' AND days_back <= 30 THEN 5
                 WHEN severity='LOW' AND days_back <= 28 THEN 1
                 ELSE 0 END) AS risk_score
    FROM sig GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT c.customer_id, c.tier, c.lifecycle_state,
    COALESCE(fs.l7_signals, 0) AS l7_signals,
    COALESCE(fs.l28_signals, 0) AS l28_signals,
    COALESCE(fs.l90_signals, 0) AS l90_signals,
    COALESCE(fs.high_l90, 0) AS high_l90,
    COALESCE(fs.medium_l30, 0) AS medium_l30,
    COALESCE(fs.low_l28, 0) AS low_l28,
    COALESCE(bp.longest_burst, 0) AS longest_burst,
    COALESCE(fs.risk_score, 0) AS risk_score,
    CASE
        WHEN COALESCE(fs.high_l90, 0) > 0
          OR COALESCE(fs.risk_score, 0) >= 15
          OR COALESCE(bp.longest_burst, 0) >= 5 THEN 'hard_excluded'
        WHEN COALESCE(fs.medium_l30, 0) > 0
          OR COALESCE(fs.risk_score, 0) >= 5
          OR COALESCE(bp.longest_burst, 0) >= 3 THEN 'manual_review'
        ELSE 'eligible'
    END AS status
FROM customers c
JOIN cmp ON c.lifecycle_state = cmp.target_state
LEFT JOIN fraud_summary fs ON fs.customer_id = c.customer_id
LEFT JOIN burst_per_cust bp ON bp.customer_id = c.customer_id
WHERE CAST(substr(c.tier, 6) AS INTEGER) <= CAST(substr(cmp.min_tier, 6) AS INTEGER)
ORDER BY risk_score DESC, c.customer_id ASC;

title: Per-Order Growth Status with Monthly Cohort + MoM Trend
subtopic: LAG · per-order classification · MoM growth · QoQ summary
schema: orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, julianday, labeled, monthly, strftime, trended, year_month, status)
scenario: The classifier alone (NEW / RESURRECTED / RETAINED_REPEAT) is useful, but the executive dashboard needs the trend: how many NEW customers this month vs last? How does Q1 of this year compare to Q4 of last year? The team wants the per-month breakdown plus a single MoM growth column for each bucket so the slide deck practically writes itself.
question:
Build a monthly report for the past 6 months where each row is
  year_month, growth_status, customers, prev_month_customers, mom_growth_pct
growth_status ∈ {'NEW','RESURRECTED','RETAINED_REPEAT'}.

  • For each order, label its growth_status using the standard
    rules (no previous order = NEW, gap > 90d = RESURRECTED,
    else RETAINED_REPEAT).
  • Aggregate distinct customers per (year_month, status).
  • Add prev_month_customers via LAG within (status partition).
  • mom_growth_pct = (cur - prev) / prev × 100; NULL if prev is 0.

Sort by year_month DESC, status priority (NEW, RESURRECTED, RETAINED_REPEAT).
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER, order_date DATE, amount NUMERIC
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (1, 1,'2024-08-04', 80),(2, 1,'2024-12-12',100),
  (3, 1,'2025-01-22',110),(4, 1,'2025-03-08',120),
  (5, 2,'2024-11-04',150),(6, 2,'2024-12-22',180),
  (7, 2,'2025-02-14',200),
  (8, 3,'2024-12-09',300),
  (9, 3,'2025-04-12',280),
  (10,4,'2025-01-15',120),(11,4,'2025-02-22',90),(12,4,'2025-03-30',110),
  (13,5,'2025-02-08', 60),
  (14,5,'2025-04-25', 80),
  (15,6,'2024-04-01', 50),(16,6,'2025-03-04', 75),
  (17,7,'2025-03-15',200),
  (18,7,'2025-04-02',220),
  (19,8,'2025-04-08',330);

WITH labeled AS (
    SELECT customer_id, order_date,
        TO_CHAR(order_date, 'YYYY-MM') AS ym,
        CASE
            WHEN LAG(order_date) OVER w IS NULL THEN 'NEW'
            WHEN (order_date - LAG(order_date) OVER w) > 90 THEN 'RESURRECTED'
            ELSE 'RETAINED_REPEAT'
        END AS growth_status
    FROM orders
    WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date)
),
monthly AS (
    SELECT ym AS year_month, growth_status,
           COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS customers
    FROM labeled
    WHERE order_date >= DATE '2024-11-01'
    GROUP BY ym, growth_status
),
trended AS (
    SELECT year_month, growth_status, customers,
           LAG(customers) OVER (PARTITION BY growth_status ORDER BY year_month) AS prev_month_customers
    FROM monthly
)
SELECT year_month, growth_status, customers, prev_month_customers,
    CASE WHEN prev_month_customers > 0
         THEN ROUND(100.0*(customers - prev_month_customers)/prev_month_customers, 1)
         END AS mom_growth_pct
FROM trended
ORDER BY year_month DESC,
    CASE growth_status WHEN 'NEW' THEN 1
                       WHEN 'RESURRECTED' THEN 2
                       ELSE 3 END;

title: Dynamic Customer Tiers via pd.qcut — Spend × Frequency
solution:
import pandas as pd


def calculate_tiers(customers: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Bucket customers into Tier 1 / 2 / 3 by combining a spend tertile and
    an order-count tertile. The tiers are *dynamic*: pd.qcut re-anchors
    the bucket edges on the current snapshot, so 'Tier 1' always means
    'top relative to the rest of this book' rather than a fixed dollar
    cut. New shoppers and seasonal lulls don't break the segmentation.

    Inputs
        customers columns: customer_id, total_spend, order_count.

    Output
        customer_id | spend_score | freq_score | tier
        Score convention: 1 = top tertile (best), 3 = bottom.

    Pro-tip
        Always pass duplicates='drop' — qcut throws on small or skewed
        inputs (e.g. when half the book has total_spend = 0 and the
        bin edges collide). 'drop' silently merges the duplicate bins
        and gets you a valid result.
    """
    df = customers.copy()

    # 1. Score each dimension independently. labels=[3, 2, 1] flips the
    #    natural qcut ordering so 1 = top, matching the tier name.
    df['spend_score'] = pd.qcut(df['total_spend'], 3,
                                labels=[3, 2, 1], duplicates='drop')
    df['freq_score']  = pd.qcut(df['order_count'], 3,
                                labels=[3, 2, 1], duplicates='drop')

    # 2. Combine. Integer division by 2 maps the [2..6] sum into [1..3];
    #    clip is paranoia for the edge case where only one qcut bucket
    #    survived dedup (e.g. all customers have the same spend).
    df['tier'] = (
        (df['spend_score'].astype(int) + df['freq_score'].astype(int)) // 2
    ).clip(lower=1, upper=3)
    df['tier'] = 'Tier ' + df['tier'].astype(str)

    # 3. Sort by tier so VIPs surface at the top of the export.
    return (df[['customer_id', 'spend_score', 'freq_score', 'tier']]
            .sort_values(['tier', 'customer_id']).reset_index(drop=True))

title: PyFlink Table API — Serial-Returner Detector with HOP Window
subtopic: Flink · Table API · HOP · sliding window
solution:
"""
PyFlink Table API job — flag accounts triggering > 3 RETURN_REQUESTED
events in any rolling 1-hour window (slide: 5 minutes). The pattern
is the standard 'serial returner' signature: refund-fraud rings
automate dozens of return submissions in a short burst.

Why HOP, not TUMBLE
    A tumbling 1-hour window resets on the hour, so a burst that
    straddles 12:55 → 13:05 splits across two buckets and never
    individually crosses the threshold. HOP (sliding) overlaps:
    every 5 minutes a fresh 1-hour window opens, so any 60-minute
    span is covered by at least one window.

Pro-tip
    The PRIMARY KEY (user_id, window_start) on the sink dedupes
    multiple HOP windows that end up flagging the same burst — your
    risk database receives one row per (user, anchor minute), not
    twelve overlapping rows for the same incident.
"""
from pyflink.table import (
    EnvironmentSettings, TableEnvironment, DataTypes
)


def build_pipeline(t_env: TableEnvironment) -> str:
    """Register a Kafka source + JDBC sink and return the alert query.
    Caller is responsible for `t_env.execute_sql(...)` against the
    returned SQL — split lets unit tests assert the SQL string before
    paying for Flink to actually run it."""
    # 1. Source — Kafka topic of every order-related event. The
    #    PROCTIME() column gives Flink a clock to window against; we
    #    prefer event time in production (this example uses processing
    #    time for brevity).
    t_env.execute_sql("""
      CREATE TABLE stream_events (
        event_id     STRING,
        user_id      BIGINT,
        event_type   STRING,
        proctime AS PROCTIME()
      ) WITH (
        'connector' = 'kafka',
        'topic'     = 'orders.events',
        'properties.bootstrap.servers' = 'kafka:9092',
        'format'    = 'json',
        'scan.startup.mode' = 'latest-offset'
      )
    """)

    # 2. Sink — JDBC upsert into Postgres. The PRIMARY KEY dedupes
    #    overlapping HOP windows that flag the same burst.
    t_env.execute_sql("""
      CREATE TABLE serial_returner_alerts (
        user_id      BIGINT,
        window_start TIMESTAMP(3),
        window_end   TIMESTAMP(3),
        return_count BIGINT,
        PRIMARY KEY (user_id, window_start) NOT ENFORCED
      ) WITH (
        'connector' = 'jdbc',
        'url'       = 'jdbc:postgresql://risk-db:5432/fraud',
        'table-name'= 'serial_returner_alerts'
      )
    """)

    # 3. The detector — HOP gives us overlapping windows; HAVING > 3
    #    fires the alert; HOP_START / HOP_END expose the window edges
    #    so the analyst sees exactly when the burst happened.
    return """
      INSERT INTO serial_returner_alerts
      SELECT
        user_id,
        HOP_START(proctime, INTERVAL '5' MINUTE, INTERVAL '1' HOUR)
          AS window_start,
        HOP_END  (proctime, INTERVAL '5' MINUTE, INTERVAL '1' HOUR)
          AS window_end,
        COUNT(event_id) AS return_count
      FROM stream_events
      WHERE event_type = 'RETURN_REQUESTED'
      GROUP BY user_id,
        HOP(proctime, INTERVAL '5' MINUTE, INTERVAL '1' HOUR)
      HAVING COUNT(event_id) > 3
    """


if __name__ == '__main__':
    settings = EnvironmentSettings.in_streaming_mode()
    env = TableEnvironment.create(settings)
    sql = build_pipeline(env)
    env.execute_sql(sql).wait()

title: Passive Customers — High-Intent vs Bored Clickstream Classifier
solution:
import pandas as pd


def passive_intent(events: pd.DataFrame, days: int = 30) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Among passive customers (no purchase in the window), separate the
    'high-intent' shoppers from the 'bored' browsers using clickstream
    signals — the same telemetry the site already collects, just read
    against a deliberate set of weights.

    Why weighted scoring
        A simple 'has add_to_cart' flag misses the customer who's
        carefully reading reviews, comparing prices, and revisiting
        the same product 4 times — high intent without ever clicking
        'Add to cart'. Weighting each event type by its observed
        purchase-correlation captures that nuance and makes the
        cohort marketable.

    Inputs
        events columns: customer_id, event_ts (datetime), event_type,
        product_id.
        event_type ∈ {'view','detail_view','add_to_cart','wishlist_add',
                      'review_read','price_check','checkout','purchase'}.

    Output
        customer_id | sessions | unique_products | revisits |
        intent_score | label
        label ∈ {'high_intent', 'considering', 'bored'}.

    Pro-tip
        A customer with revisits >= 3 to ONE product is a stronger
        signal than the same intent_score spread across many
        products. The +1 bonus below captures that without doubling
        the dimensionality of the score.
    """
    # 1. Score weights — calibrate these on your funnel; the values
    #    below are decent defaults derived from 'how often does this
    #    event precede a purchase within 7 days'.
    weights = {
        'view':         0.1,
        'detail_view':  0.3,
        'review_read':  0.5,
        'wishlist_add': 1.5,
        'price_check':  1.5,
        'add_to_cart':  2.0,
        'checkout':     4.0,
    }

    # 2. Window the recent activity, anchored on the latest event.
    df = events.copy()
    df['event_ts'] = pd.to_datetime(df['event_ts'])
    cutoff = df['event_ts'].max() - pd.Timedelta(days=days)
    recent = df[df['event_ts'] >= cutoff]

    # 3. Drop customers who actually purchased — they're not passive.
    buyers = set(recent.loc[recent['event_type'] == 'purchase',
                            'customer_id'].unique())
    passive = recent[~recent['customer_id'].isin(buyers)].copy()
    passive['weight'] = passive['event_type'].map(weights).fillna(0)

    # 4. Per-customer aggregation: session count, breadth, and the
    #    weighted intent score (sum of per-event weights).
    base = (passive.groupby('customer_id')
                   .agg(sessions=('event_ts', 'nunique'),
                        unique_products=('product_id', 'nunique'),
                        intent_score=('weight', 'sum'))
                   .reset_index())

    # 5. Revisit bonus — anyone with >= 3 hits on the SAME product
    #    earns +1 intent. Strong deliberation signal that pure
    #    event-weight summing misses.
    revisits = (passive.groupby(['customer_id', 'product_id']).size()
                       .reset_index(name='hits'))
    revisit_n = (revisits[revisits['hits'] >= 3]
                 .groupby('customer_id').size()
                 .reset_index(name='revisits'))
    out = base.merge(revisit_n, on='customer_id', how='left')
    out['revisits'] = out['revisits'].fillna(0).astype(int)
    out['intent_score'] = (out['intent_score']
                           + (out['revisits'] > 0).astype(int) * 1.0).round(2)

    # 6. Three-band labelling. 5.0 / 2.0 are tunable thresholds —
    #    re-fit them per quarter as the funnel changes.
    def _label(s):
        if s >= 5.0: return 'high_intent'
        if s >= 2.0: return 'considering'
        return 'bored'
    out['label'] = out['intent_score'].apply(_label)

    return (out[['customer_id','sessions','unique_products',
                 'revisits','intent_score','label']]
            .sort_values('intent_score', ascending=False)
            .reset_index(drop=True))

title: Undo a Push to the Wrong Branch
solution:
# 1. From your local main (which has the bad commits), branch off a feature branch
git checkout main
git switch -c feature/wrong-push          # captures current commits

# 2. Find the previous good commit on main (before your push)
git log --oneline origin/main             # or check the GitHub compare view
GOOD_SHA=<previous-good-sha>

# 3. Reset local main to that commit
git checkout main
git reset --hard $GOOD_SHA

# 4. Force-push main back to origin (coordinate with team first!)
git push --force-with-lease origin main

# 5. Push the feature branch where the work belongs
git push -u origin feature/wrong-push

# Tip: --force-with-lease is safer than --force — it refuses if someone else
# has pushed in the meantime, preventing you from clobbering their work.
question: You ran `git push origin main` but the work was meant for a feature branch. Nothing else has been pushed to main since. How do you (a) move the commits to a new feature branch, (b) restore main to its previous state on origin, and (c) leave your local working tree intact?

title: Recover a Deleted Local Branch
solution:
# Yes — git keeps a record of every HEAD position in the reflog for ~90 days.

# 1. Find the branch's last tip SHA
git reflog | grep -i "payments"
# Look for a line like: 9a3b1c2 HEAD@{12}: checkout: moving from feature/payments to main

# 2. Re-create the branch at that SHA
git branch feature/payments 9a3b1c2

# 3. Verify the work is back
git log feature/payments --oneline -10

# Note: reflog is local-only and per-clone. If you'd run `git gc --prune=now`
# the unreachable commits could be gone — but defaults keep them ~30-90 days.
question: You ran `git branch -D feature/payments` and immediately realized it had unmerged work. The branch tip was never pushed. Can you get it back?

title: Squash Last 5 Commits Before Opening a PR
solution:
# Option A — interactive rebase (the canonical way)
git rebase -i HEAD~5
# In the editor, leave the first line as 'pick' and change the next 4 to 'squash'
# (or 'fixup' to discard their messages). Save and exit.
# Git then opens a second editor for the combined commit message — write a
# clean summary and close.

# Option B — soft reset + single commit (faster when you want a fresh message)
git reset --soft HEAD~5
git commit -m "Add payments retry policy with exponential backoff"

# Force-push to update the PR branch
git push --force-with-lease

# Tip: if the branch is already in a PR review, prefer 'fixup' commits during
# review and squash on merge — easier for reviewers to track changes.
question: Your feature branch has 5 messy WIP commits. Before opening the PR, consolidate them into one clean commit with a proper message — without losing any code changes.

title: Recover Commits After Accidental git reset --hard
solution:
# git reset --hard moves the branch pointer but doesn't immediately delete
# the orphaned commits — they live in the reflog until garbage collection.

# 1. Find the pre-reset SHA in the reflog
git reflog
# Look for: abc1234 HEAD@{1}: reset: moving to HEAD~3
# The SHA on the line ABOVE that (HEAD@{0} before reset) is what you want
# Or look for: def5678 HEAD@{2}: commit: Your last commit message

# 2. Reset hard back to the recovered SHA
git reset --hard def5678

# 3. Confirm
git log --oneline -10

# Belt-and-braces: if you're paranoid, create a recovery branch first
git branch recovery-2026-05-06 def5678
# then verify before resetting your real branch.
question: You ran `git reset --hard HEAD~3` thinking you were on a scratch branch — but it was your feature branch and those 3 commits had unpushed work. The branch is local only. Recover the commits.

title: Cherry-Pick a Hotfix from main into a Release Branch
solution:
# 1. Switch to the release branch and make sure it's up to date
git checkout release/v2.4
git pull origin release/v2.4

# 2. Cherry-pick the single commit
git cherry-pick a1b2c3d

# If the fix touched files that have diverged on the release branch, you'll
# get a conflict. Resolve, then continue:
# git status                # see conflicted files
# (edit files to resolve)
# git add <files>
# git cherry-pick --continue

# 3. Push the hotfix
git push origin release/v2.4

# Tip: for a multi-commit fix, cherry-pick a range:
#   git cherry-pick a1b2c3d^..f9e8d7c
# The '^' makes the start inclusive.
question: A bug fix was merged into main as commit `a1b2c3d`. The release/v2.4 branch froze last week and only takes hotfixes. Bring the fix into release/v2.4 without merging in any of main's other recent commits.

title: Find Which Commit Introduced a Bug (git bisect)
solution:
# git bisect runs a binary search across commits — O(log n) checkouts.

# 1. Start the bisect session
git bisect start
git bisect bad                    # current HEAD (main) is broken
git bisect good v1.0.0            # tag/SHA where tests passed

# git checks out the midpoint commit. Run your test:
pytest tests/test_payments.py
# If it passes:
git bisect good
# If it fails:
git bisect bad
# Repeat — git narrows the range each step.

# 2. When done, git prints: "<sha> is the first bad commit"
git bisect reset                  # return to your original branch

# Automate it — let git run the test for you:
git bisect start HEAD v1.0.0
git bisect run pytest tests/test_payments.py
# Exit code 0 = good, non-zero = bad. git pinpoints the commit, no manual loop.
question: Tests pass on commit `v1.0.0` but fail on `main` (200 commits later). Find the exact commit that introduced the regression.

title: Rewrite Author Email on the Last 3 Commits
solution:
# Set the right identity going forward (do this first so new commits are clean)
git config user.email "you@work.com"

# Rewrite the last 3 commits' author/committer
git rebase -i HEAD~3 --exec 'git commit --amend --reset-author --no-edit'
# In the editor, leave all lines as 'pick' and save — the --exec runs after
# each commit is replayed, rewriting it with the current user.email.

# Verify
git log --format='%h %ae %s' -3

# If you'd already pushed, you'd need a force-push; coordinate with the team
# because rewriting public history breaks everyone else's clones.
question: You committed 3 commits with your personal email but they should show your work email. Nothing has been pushed yet. Fix the author on those commits.

title: Sync a Fork with the Upstream Repository
solution:
# 1. Add the upstream remote (one-time setup)
git remote add upstream https://github.com/org/project.git
git remote -v                      # confirm: origin = your fork, upstream = original

# 2. Fetch upstream's latest
git fetch upstream

# 3. Switch to local main and fast-forward to upstream/main
git checkout main
git merge --ff-only upstream/main
# --ff-only refuses if your main has diverged — forcing you to handle it
# explicitly (rebase or merge), instead of silently creating a merge commit.

# 4. Push the synced main to your fork
git push origin main

# Pro tip: GitHub now has a 'Sync fork' button on the repo page that does
# this in the UI — handy when you're away from a clone.
question: Your fork of `org/project` is 47 commits behind upstream/main. Sync your fork's main branch with upstream and push the update to your fork on GitHub.

title: Resolve a deleted-by-them, modified-by-us Conflict
solution:
# Two valid resolutions — pick one based on intent:

# A) Keep the file (your edits matter, the deletion was wrong)
git add src/legacy.py
git commit                          # finalize the merge

# B) Honor the deletion (the file is going away — discard your edits)
git rm src/legacy.py
git commit

# Common gotcha: 'git status' shows it as 'deleted by them' AND 'modified by
# us'. Plain 'git add' won't resolve it because git doesn't know which side
# you mean. You MUST either explicitly 'git add' (keep) or 'git rm' (delete).

# To inspect what was modified before deciding:
git log --all --oneline -- src/legacy.py    # who deleted, who edited?
git show HEAD:src/legacy.py | less          # see your version
git show MERGE_HEAD:src/legacy.py 2>&1      # error: file is gone in their branch
question: During a merge, git reports `CONFLICT (modify/delete): src/legacy.py deleted in upstream and modified in HEAD`. The other branch deleted the file; you edited it. Decide and resolve.

title: Update Remote URL After Repo Rename or Org Move
solution:
# 1. See the current remote
git remote -v
# origin  https://github.com/acme/dashboard.git (fetch)
# origin  https://github.com/acme/dashboard.git (push)

# 2. Set the new URL
git remote set-url origin https://github.com/acme/insights-dashboard.git

# (SSH form — preferred if you use SSH keys)
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:acme/insights-dashboard.git

# 3. Verify and refetch
git remote -v
git fetch origin

# Note: GitHub does redirect old URLs for a while, so pushes often keep
# working silently after a rename. But it's good hygiene to update — the
# redirect is not guaranteed forever, and 'git remote -v' lying about your
# real remote causes confusion later.
question: Your team renamed the GitHub repo from `acme/dashboard` to `acme/insights-dashboard` (or moved it to a new org). Push now fails with `remote: Repository not found`. Update your local clone's remote URL.

title: Parse JSONL and Save Top 10 Categories to CSV
solution:
import json, csv
from collections import Counter

counts = Counter()
with open('events.jsonl') as f:
    for line in f:
        try:
            obj = json.loads(line)
        except json.JSONDecodeError:
            continue                    # skip corrupted lines, don't crash
        cat = obj.get('category')
        if cat:
            counts[cat] += 1

with open('top10.csv', 'w', newline='') as out:
    w = csv.writer(out)
    w.writerow(['category', 'count'])
    for cat, n in counts.most_common(10):
        w.writerow([cat, n])

# Why streaming: Counter is O(unique-categories) memory regardless of file
# size. Loading json.loads(f.read()) on a 2 GB file would OOM most laptops.
question: You have a 2 GB file `events.jsonl` (one JSON object per line, each with a `category` field). Stream-parse it (don't load it all into memory), count events per category, and write the top 10 categories to `top10.csv`.

title: Retry an HTTP Request with Exponential Backoff
solution:
import requests, time, random

def get_with_retry(url, max_attempts=5, base=1.0, timeout=10):
    for attempt in range(max_attempts):
        try:
            r = requests.get(url, timeout=timeout)
            if r.status_code < 500:     # 2xx, 3xx, 4xx — return as-is
                return r
            last_err = f"HTTP {r.status_code}"
        except requests.RequestException as e:
            last_err = str(e)

        if attempt == max_attempts - 1:
            raise RuntimeError(f"Failed after {max_attempts} attempts: {last_err}")

        sleep = base * (2 ** attempt) + random.uniform(0, 0.5)   # +jitter
        time.sleep(sleep)

# In production, prefer urllib3's Retry adapter — it's battle-tested and
# integrates with requests' Session. The above is the from-scratch version
# you'd write in an interview where importing extras isn't allowed.
question: Wrap a `requests.get(url)` call so it retries up to 5 times on connection errors and 5xx responses, with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s) and a tiny bit of jitter so 1000 clients don't retry in lockstep.

title: Watch a Directory for New Files (No Dependencies)
solution:
import os, time
from pathlib import Path

WATCH_DIR = Path('/data/incoming')

def watch(dir_path, interval=5.0):
    seen = set(p.name for p in dir_path.iterdir())
    print(f"watching {dir_path} ({len(seen)} files at startup)")
    while True:
