    (2, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    (3, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 32400, TRUE),
    (3, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-03', 32400, TRUE),
    -- Dolphins (users 4-13): 5-10 hours each
    (4, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    (5, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 28800, TRUE),
    (6, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-03', 25200, TRUE),
    (7, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-04', 21600, TRUE),
    (8, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-05', 21600, TRUE),
    (9,  'Black Mirror',   '2025-04-01', 18000, TRUE),
    (10, 'The Witcher',    '2025-04-02', 18000, TRUE),
    (11, 'Bridgerton',     '2025-04-03', 14400, TRUE),
    (12, 'Stranger Things','2025-04-04', 14400, TRUE),
    (13, 'Black Mirror',   '2025-04-05', 10800, TRUE),
    -- Minnows (users 14-20): 0.5-3 hours each
    (14, 'Stranger Things','2025-04-01', 10800, TRUE),
    (15, 'The Witcher',    '2025-04-02', 7200,  TRUE),
    (16, 'Bridgerton',     '2025-04-03', 5400,  TRUE),
    (17, 'Stranger Things','2025-04-04', 3600,  TRUE),
    (18, 'Black Mirror',   '2025-04-05', 1800,  TRUE),
    (19, 'The Witcher',    '2025-04-01', 900,   TRUE),
    (20, 'Bridgerton',     '2025-04-02', 600,   TRUE),
    -- LAD-incomplete row — would otherwise look like a minnow (under-counted hours)
    -- and corrupt the deciles. The is_metadata_complete=FALSE filter excludes it.
    (99, NULL,             '2025-04-05', 18000, FALSE);

-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  ARCHETYPE CLASSIFICATION + SKEW SIGNALS                          │
-- │    • whale         = NTILE(20) bucket 1     (top 5%)              │
-- │    • heavy_dolphin = NTILE(10) buckets 2-3                        │
-- │    • dolphin       = NTILE(10) buckets 4-8                        │
-- │    • minnow        = NTILE(10) buckets 9-10 (bottom 20%)          │
-- │  Global stats:                                                    │
-- │    skew_ratio = AVG / MEDIAN  (1.0 = uniform; >2 = Pareto)       │
-- │    p99_over_p50 = 99th-pct / median  (interview-friendly variant) │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH user_metrics AS (
    SELECT user_id, SUM(watch_seconds) / 3600.0 AS hours
    FROM viewing_activity
    WHERE is_metadata_complete = TRUE   -- LAD guard
    GROUP BY user_id
),
global_stats AS (
    SELECT
        AVG(hours)                                              AS mean_hours,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5)  WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY hours)     AS median_hours,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY hours)     AS p99_hours,
        COUNT(*)                                                AS total_users
    FROM user_metrics
),
classified AS (
    SELECT
        user_id, hours,
        CASE
            WHEN NTILE(20) OVER (ORDER BY hours DESC) = 1     THEN 'whale'
            WHEN NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY hours DESC) BETWEEN 2 AND 3 THEN 'heavy_dolphin'
            WHEN NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY hours DESC) BETWEEN 4 AND 8 THEN 'dolphin'
            ELSE                                                   'minnow'
        END AS archetype
    FROM user_metrics
)
SELECT
    c.archetype,
    COUNT(*)                                                AS user_count,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) / (SELECT total_users FROM global_stats), 1)
                                                            AS pct_of_users,
    ROUND(AVG(c.hours)::NUMERIC, 2)                          AS avg_hours_per_user,
    ROUND(SUM(c.hours)::NUMERIC, 2)                          AS total_hours,
    ROUND(((SELECT mean_hours FROM global_stats) /
           NULLIF((SELECT median_hours FROM global_stats), 0))::NUMERIC, 2)
                                                            AS skew_ratio_mean_over_median,
    ROUND(((SELECT p99_hours FROM global_stats) /
           NULLIF((SELECT median_hours FROM global_stats), 0))::NUMERIC, 2)
                                                            AS p99_over_p50
FROM classified c
GROUP BY c.archetype
ORDER BY
    CASE c.archetype
        WHEN 'whale'         THEN 1
        WHEN 'heavy_dolphin' THEN 2
        WHEN 'dolphin'       THEN 3
        WHEN 'minnow'        THEN 4
    END;

title: Dynamic Pivot — The Question That Has No Clean Answer (JSON Aggregation + Three Workarounds)
subtopic: jsonb_object_agg · OBJECT_AGG · PIVOT ANY · long-format · dbt get_column_values · schema-on-read
schema: employees(emp_id, name, salary, department, email, hire_date, dept_id, manager_id, job_title)
scenario: A Netflix-style L5 trap. The interviewer asks: "List each department's highest and second-highest salary as columns — and if a new department appears tomorrow, the query must auto-add a column." The honest answer is that standard SQL CANNOT truly do dynamic pivoting, because column names must be known at query-parse time. Every "dynamic pivot" is one of three workarounds: (1) two-step dynamic SQL string-building, (2) keep the result long-format and pivot in the consumer, (3) JSON-object aggregation with stable outer schema and evolving inner keys. The senior signal is recognising that a request for dynamic pivot is usually a request to push presentation logic OUT of SQL.
question:
Using employees(emp_id, name, salary, department), produce a 2-row result with the highest and second-highest salary per department. The catch: the department list isn't known upfront — adding 'Legal' tomorrow must NOT require a query rewrite. Use the schema-stable JSON-aggregation approach (jsonb_object_agg in Postgres / JSON_OBJECTAGG in MySQL 8 / OBJECT_AGG in Snowflake): the outer schema stays at 2 columns (salary_position, by_department), and the inner JSON object's keys evolve with the data.

Then in the enrichment compare with the other two approaches (two-step dynamic SQL, long-format + client pivot) and the production patterns (dbt get_column_values, schema-on-read, event-time dim registry).
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS employees;
CREATE TABLE employees (
    emp_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name        TEXT NOT NULL,
    salary      NUMERIC NOT NULL,
    department  TEXT NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
    (1, 'Alex',   220000, 'Engineering'),
    (2, 'Bailey', 195000, 'Engineering'),
    (3, 'Casey',  180000, 'Engineering'),
    (4, 'Drew',   170000, 'Sales'),
    (5, 'Ellis',  150000, 'Sales'),
    (6, 'Finn',   140000, 'Marketing'),
    (7, 'Gray',   130000, 'Marketing'),
    (8, 'Harper', 125000, 'HR'),
    (9, 'Indy',   115000, 'HR');

-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  THE JSON-AGGREGATION ANSWER (the only truly schema-stable form)  │
-- │   Outer columns:  salary_position, by_department                  │
-- │   Inner keys:     evolve with the data — no DDL when 'Legal'      │
-- │                   appears tomorrow                                │
-- │   Portable:       Postgres jsonb_object_agg                       │
-- │                   Snowflake OBJECT_AGG                            │
-- │                   MySQL 8 JSON_OBJECTAGG                          │
-- │                   BigQuery TO_JSON_STRING(ARRAY_AGG(...))         │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH RankedSalaries AS (
    SELECT department, salary,
        DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rnk
    FROM employees
)
SELECT
    CASE WHEN rnk = 1 THEN '1st Highest'
         WHEN rnk = 2 THEN '2nd Highest' END                    AS salary_position,
    jsonb_object_agg(department, salary ORDER BY department)    AS by_department
FROM RankedSalaries
WHERE rnk IN (1, 2)
GROUP BY rnk
ORDER BY rnk;
